name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
وَإِذْ قَالَ ٱللَّهُ يَـٰعِيسَى ٱبْنَ مَرْيَمَ ءَأَنتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ ٱتَّخِذُونِى وَأُمِّىَ إِلَـٰهَيْنِ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ قَالَ سُبْحَـٰنَكَ مَا يَكُونُ لِىٓ أَنْ أَقُولَ مَا لَيْسَ لِى بِحَقٍّ ۚ إِن كُنتُ قُلْتُهُۥ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُۥ ۚ تَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِى وَلَآ أَعْلَمُ مَا فِى نَفْسِكَ ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ عَلَّـٰمُ ٱلْغُيُوبِ (١١٦)
```

```english
And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary! Was it you who said to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for gods besides Allah’’?’ He will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say]. Had I said it, You would certainly have known it: You know whatever is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self. Indeed, You are knower of all that is Unseen. (116)
```

```arabic
مَا قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا مَآ أَمَرْتَنِى بِهِۦٓ أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۚ وَكُنتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيدًا مَّا دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ ۖ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِى كُنتَ أَنتَ ٱلرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ وَأَنتَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ شَهِيدٌ (١١٧)
```

```english
I did not say to them [anything] except what You had commanded me [to say]: ‘‘Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord.’’ And I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had taken me away, You Yourself were watchful over them, and You are witness to all things. (117)
```

```arabic
إِن تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبَادُكَ ۖ وَإِن تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَإِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (١١٨)
```

```english
If You punish them, they are indeed Your creatures; but if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise.’ (118)
```

```arabic
قَالَ ٱللَّهُ هَـٰذَا يَوْمُ يَنفَعُ ٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ صِدْقُهُمْ ۚ لَهُمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَآ أَبَدًا ۚ رَّضِىَ ٱللَّهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا۟ عَنْهُ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْعَظِيمُ (١١٩)
```

```english
Allah will say, ‘This day truthfulness shall benefit the truthful. For them there will be gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them forever. Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him. That is the great success.’ (119)
```

```arabic
لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا فِيهِنَّ ۚ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌۢ (١٢٠)
```

```english
To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever there is in them, and He has power over all things. (120)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the direct address of Allah to His Messenger, Jesus, son of Mary, regarding what the Christians said concerning him. The purpose of narrating these verses is to mention what he (peace be upon him) confessed and related about himself during his life in this world: that it was not his right to claim for himself what was not his, for he was under the ever-watchful eye of Allah that neither sleeps nor deviates, and that he did not exceed the limit that Allah, the Glorified, set for him. Thus, he said nothing except what he was commanded to say, and he occupied himself with the task Allah charged him with, which was the matter of bearing witness (الشهادة). Allah, the Exalted, has confirmed him in what he mentioned regarding the right of Lordship (الربوبية) and servitude (العبودية).

In this way, the verses align with the purpose for which the Surah was revealed, which is to clarify the right established for Allah over His servants: that they fulfill the covenant they made and not break the pledge (الميثاق). They have no right to act as they please or to indulge freely wherever they wish, for they do not possess this kind of right from their Lord, nor are they capable of it by themselves. **لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما فِيهِنَّ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** "To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is in them, and He has power over all things" (5:120). With this, the Surah concludes.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذْ قالَ اللهُ يا عِيسَى ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ ، أَأَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّي إِلهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "And when Allah will say, ‘O Jesus son of Mary! Was it you who said to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah’’?’" (5:116). The word "when" (إِذْ) is an adverbial particle (ظرف) relating to a deleted verb indicated by the context. What is meant here is the Day of Resurrection, due to His saying, the Exalted, therein: **قالَ اللهُ هذا يَوْمُ يَنْفَعُ الصَّادِقِينَ صِدْقُهُمْ** "Allah will say, ‘This is a day when the truthful will benefit from their truthfulness’" (5:119), and the saying of Jesus (peace be upon him) therein: **وَكُنْتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً ما دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ** "I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had me taken up, You were Yourself the watcher over them" (5:117).

The verse refers to Mary by her motherhood, saying: "Take me and my mother for two gods," rather than saying "Take me and Mary for two gods," to point toward the core of their argument for divinity—which is his birth from her without a father. This specific sonship and motherhood are the basis for that claim; thus, the expression "him and his mother" is more indicative and eloquent than the expression "Jesus and Mary."

The word "besides" (دُونِ) is used, according to the ultimate meaning, to signify "other than." Al-Raghib said: It is said of that which falls short of something as being "below" (دون). Some have said it is a metathesis of "closeness" (الدنو), and the "lower" (الأدون) is the "vile" (الدني). His saying, the Exalted: **لا تَتَّخِذُوا بِطانَةً مِنْ دُونِكُمْ** "do not take as confidants anyone besides yourselves" (3:118) means those who have not reached your level in religion, or it is said, in kinship. His saying: **وَيَغْفِرُ ما دُونَ ذلِكَ** "and He forgives what is less than that" (4:48) means what is less than that, or it is said, what is other than that; the two meanings are inseparable. His saying: **أَأَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّي إِلهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** means "other than Allah." End quote.

The phrase "besides Allah" (مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ) is used frequently in the Quran to mean association (الإشراك) rather than total independence. This means that taking one god, two gods, or many gods "besides Allah" implies taking something other than Allah as a partner to Allah, the Glorified, in His divinity (ألوهيته), not that one takes another as a god while denying the divinity of Allah, the Glorified. Such a denial would be nonsensical speech that leads to no coherent result; for whatever one affirms at that point would be the "God," the Glorified, while denying everything else. The dispute would then return to certain attributes that were affirmed. For example, if someone said, "The God is the Messiah," and denied the God of the Messiah, the substance of his speech would return to affirming the Exalted God but describing Him with the human attributes of the Messiah. Similarly, if someone said that idols or the lords of idols are gods and denied Allah, the Exalted and Sanctified, he is still saying that the world has a god; thus, he has affirmed Allah, the Glorified, but described Him with the attribute of multiplicity and plurality, thereby assigning partners to Allah. Or he might say, as the Christians do: **إِنَّ اللهَ ثالِثُ ثَلاثَةٍ** "Allah is the third [person] of a trinity" (5:73), meaning one is three and three is one.

Whoever says that the origin of the world is time (الدهر) or nature (الطبيعة) and denies that the world has a God—Exalted is He above that—has affirmed a Maker for the world, who is Allah, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه), but has described Him with attributes of deficiency, lack, and contingency (الإمكان).

And whoever denies that this wondrous system has any origin at all, and denies causality (العلية) and influence (التأثير) despite the clear dictates of his primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — has affirmed a world that is existent and fixed, not accepting non-existence from the start. That is, it is "Necessary in its Being" (واجب الثبوت), and the preserver of its stability and existence is either itself—and it is not subject to cessation or change in its parts—or it is something else, which is Allah, Blessed and Exalted, and to Him belong His attributes of perfection.

Thus, it becomes clear that Allah, the Glorified, does not accept denial at all except in outward speech, without it having any intelligible meaning.

The criterion in all of this is that man only affirms the Exalted God because of the general need in the world for One who sustains its existence and manages its order. Then he affirms the specificities of His existence. Whatever he affirms to fill this gap and remove that need is Allah, the Glorified. Then, if he affirms a god other than Him or affirms multiplicity, he has either erred in identifying His attributes and deviated (ألحد) regarding His names, or he has affirmed for Him a partner or partners—Exalted is He above that. As for denying Him and affirming another, it is meaningless.

It is thus evident that the meaning of His saying: "two gods besides Allah" (إِلهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ) is two partners to Allah who are other than Him. Even if we concede that the word does not convey the meaning of partnership (الشركة) in any way, we say: its meaning does not exceed the taking of two gods who are of a genus other than Allah, the Glorified. As for whether this is accompanied by the denial of His divinity, the Exalted, or its affirmation, the text is silent on this; it is known from external context. The Christians do not deny His divinity, the Exalted, despite taking the Messiah and his mother as two gods besides Allah, the Glorified.

Some have raised an objection to the verse, stating that the Christians do not claim divinity for the Virgin Mary (peace be upon her), and they mentioned various interpretations to justify this.

However, what must be noted is that the verse only mentions their "taking" her as a god; it does not mention their "saying" she is a god in terms of naming. Taking a god is different from the verbal claim of divinity, except by way of implication. Taking a god is validated by worship (العبادة) and servile submission (الخضوع العبودي). The Exalted said: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخِذُ إِلهَهُ هَواهُ** "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god?" (45:23). This meaning is narrated from the ancestors of the Christians and witnessed among their descendants.

Al-Alusi said in *Ruh al-Ma'ani*: Abu Ja'far al-Imami related from some Christians that in the past there was a group called the "Maryists" (المريمية) who believed that Mary was a god.

It is said in *Tafsir al-Manar*: As for their taking the Messiah as a god, this has been discussed in several places in the commentary of this Surah. As for his mother, her worship was agreed upon in the Eastern and Western churches after Constantine. Then her worship was denied by the Protestant sect, which emerged several centuries after Islam. (1)

This worship that the Christians direct toward Mary, the mother of the Messiah (peace be upon him), includes prayers consisting of supplication, praise, seeking help, and intercession. It also includes fasting attributed to her and named after her. All of this is coupled with submission and humility before her mention, her images, and her statues, and the belief in a supernatural power (سلطة غيبية) for her by which she can, in their belief, benefit or harm in this world and the Hereafter, either by herself or through her son. They have explicitly stated the obligation of worshiping her, though it is not known that any of their sects apply the word "God" (إله) to her; rather, they call her "Mother of God," and some of their sects state that this is literal, not metaphorical.

The Quran says here: they took her and her mother as two gods. "Taking" is different from "naming"; thus, it is validated by worship, which certainly occurs. In another verse, it is clarified that they said: "Allah is the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary," which is a different meaning. The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) explained the saying of the Exalted regarding the People of the Book: **اتَّخِذُوا أَحْبارَهُمْ وَرُهْبانَهُمْ أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** "They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah" (9:31), saying that they followed them in what they made lawful and prohibited, not that they named them "lords."

The first explicit text I saw regarding the Christians' worship of Mary as true worship was in the book *al-Sawa'i*, one of the books of the Greek Orthodox. I came across this book in a monastery called "Dayr al-Tilmid" when I was first entering educational institutions. The Catholic sects state this explicitly and take pride in it.

The Jesuits in Beirut decorated the ninth issue of the seventh year of their magazine *al-Mashriq* with her image and colored engravings, making it a memorial for the fiftieth anniversary of Pope Pius IX's declaration: "The Virgin Mary was conceived without the stain of sin." They established in this issue the Eastern churches' worship of Mary, just like the Western churches.

Among this is the saying of Father Louis Cheikho in an article of his therein about the Eastern churches: "That the Armenian Church worships the pure Virgin, the Mother of God, is a well-known matter." And his saying: "The Coptic Church is distinguished by its worship of the blessed Virgin, the Mother of God." End of his words.

He also quoted part of an article by Father Anastas the Carmelite, published in the fourteenth issue of the fifth year of the Catholic magazine *al-Mashriq* in Beirut. He said under the title "The Antiquity of Devotion to the Virgin," after mentioning the phrase from the Book of Genesis regarding the enmity of the serpent toward the woman and her offspring, and interpreting the woman as the Virgin: "Do you not see that you find nothing in this text that highlights the Virgin clearly until that great living prophet 'Elijah' came and brought the worship of the Virgin out from the realm of symbolism and ambiguity into the world of explicitness and clarity."

Then he interpreted this explicitness and clarity by what is in the Third Book of Kings (according to the Catholic division): that Elijah, while he was with his servant at the top of Carmel, commanded him seven times to look toward the sea. The servant informed him after looking the seventh time: that he saw a cloud the size of a man's palm rising from the sea.

The author of the article said: From that "rising" (the first formation of the cloud) (1)—I say: it is nothing but the image of Mary as the commentators have verified, and even the image of the conception without original stain. Then he said: This is the origin of the worship of the Virgin in the beloved East, and it dates back to the tenth century before Christ. The credit for that goes back to this great prophet Elijah. Then he said: Therefore, the ancestors of the Carmelites were also the first to believe in the God Jesus after the apostles and disciples, and the first to establish a temple for the Virgin after her transition to heaven in soul and body. End quote. (2)

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ سُبْحانَكَ ما يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أَقُولَ ما لَيْسَ لِي بِحَقٍّ** "He will say, ‘Immaculate are You! It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say]’" (5:116) to the end of the verse. This verse and the one following it are the response of the Messiah, Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him), to what he was asked, and he (peace be upon him) displayed therein a wondrous etiquette (أدب).

He began by glorifying Him (التسبيح), the Exalted, because he was suddenly confronted with the mention of what is not fitting to attribute to the presence of Majesty and Greatness—namely, people taking two gods besides Allah as partners to Him, the Glorified. It is part of the etiquette of servitude (أدب العبودية) that the servant glorifies his Lord when he hears what should not be heard concerning Him, the Exalted, or what occurs to the mind as a conceptualization of that. Upon this, the divine discipline has proceeded in His speech, such as His saying: **وَقالُوا اتَّخِذَ الرَّحْمنُ وَلَداً سُبْحانَهُ** "And they say, ‘The All-beneficent has taken a son.’ Immaculate is He!" (21:26) and His saying: **وَيَجْعَلُونَ لِلَّهِ الْبَناتِ سُبْحانَهُ** "They ascribe daughters to Allah—immaculate is He!" (16:57).

Then he returned to denying what he was questioned about regarding its attribution to him—that he had said to the people, "Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah." He did not deny it directly, but rather by denying its cause, as an exaggeration in sanctification. If he had said, "I did not say that" or "I did not do it," it would have contained an allusion to the possibility of its occurrence from him, though he did not do it. But when he denied it by denying its cause, saying: "It does not behoove me to say what I have no right to [say]," it was a denial of that upon which the saying depends—which is that he should have the right to say that truthfully. Denying this right is a denial of what branches from it in a more eloquent manner. This is similar to when a master says to his servant: "Why did you do what I did not command you to do?" If the servant answers by saying: "I did not do it," it is a denial of what is suspected to have occurred. But if he says: "I am too weak for that," it is a denial by denying the cause, which is power, and a rejection of its very possibility, let alone its occurrence.

His saying: **ما يَكُونُ لِي أَنْ أَقُولَ ما لَيْسَ لِي بِحَقٍّ** — if the word "be" (يَكُونُ) is incomplete (ناقصة), then its subject (اسمها) is his saying: "that I say" (أَنْ أَقُولَ) and its predicate (خبرها) is his saying: "for me" (لِي), where the "L" (اللام) denotes possession. The meaning is: "I do not possess what I do not own, and it is not my right to speak without right." If it is complete (تامة), then the word "for me" (لِي) relates to it, and his saying: "that I say, etc." is its subject (فاعل). The meaning is: "It does not happen for me to speak without right." The first of the two views is closer. In any case, the speech signifies the denial of the act by denying its cause.

His saying (peace be upon him): **إِنْ كُنْتُ قُلْتُهُ فَقَدْ عَلِمْتَهُ** "Had I said it, You would certainly have known it" (5:116) is another denial of the questioned saying, not a denial of itself by itself, but by denying its necessary consequence (لازمه). For the necessary consequence of the occurrence of this saying is that Allah would know it, because He is the One from whom nothing is hidden in the earth or in the heaven, and He is the Maintainer of every soul as to what it earns, encompassing all things.

This speech from him (peace be upon him) contains, firstly, the benefit of presenting the statement with the proof, without sufficing with a mere claim. Secondly, it intimates that what he considered in his actions and words was the knowledge of Allah, the Glorified, without paying heed to anyone else among His creation, whether they knew or were ignorant; he has no business with them.

In other words, a question is naturally valid regarding what is a suspected area of ignorance, so it is intended to deny ignorance and provide knowledge—either for the questioner himself if he is ignorant of the reality of the matter, or for someone else if the questioner is knowledgeable and wants to inform others of what he knows of the reality, as is the case with the type of question occurring in His speech, the Exalted. His saying (peace be upon him) in the response in such a context: "Had I said it, You would certainly have known it" refers the matter back to His knowledge, the Exalted, and intimates that he does not consider anything in his actions and words other than His knowledge, the Exalted.

Then he pointed by his saying: **تَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِي وَلا أَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِكَ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ** "You know what is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self. Indeed You are the Knower of all that is Unseen" (5:116) to be a sanctification of His knowledge, the Exalted, from being mixed with ignorance. Although this is also praise in itself, it is not the primary intent, because the context is not one of praise but of disavowal (التبري) from the attribution of what was attributed to him.

So his saying (peace be upon him): "You know what is in my self" is a clarification of the penetration of the knowledge he mentioned in his saying: "Had I said it, You would certainly have known it." It is an explanation that His knowledge, the Exalted, of our deeds—and He is the True King on that Day—is not like the knowledge of kings among us regarding the states of their subjects through the reports of the kingdom reaching them, so they know some things and are ignorant of others, and they are present to the state of some and heedless of the state of others. Rather, He, the Glorified, is All-Attentive (لطيف), All-Aware (خبير) of everything, including the self of Jesus son of Mary specifically.

Despite this, he did not exhaust the right of clarification in describing His knowledge, the Exalted; for He, the Glorified, knows everything, not like the knowledge of one of us regarding the state of another and the other's knowledge of his state. Rather, He knows what He knows by encompassing (الإحاطة) it, without anything encompassing Him, and they do not encompass Him in knowledge. For He, the Exalted, is a God who is non-limited (غير محدود), while everyone besides Him is limited and measured (محدود مقدر), not exceeding the bounds of his limited self. Therefore, he (peace be upon him) joined to the sentence another sentence, saying: "You know what is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self."

As for his saying: "Indeed You are the Knower of all that is Unseen," it contains the explanation of the cause for his saying: "You know what is in my self, etc." It also exhausts the right of clarification from another aspect, which is removing the illusion that the ruling of knowledge in his saying: "You know what is in my self, and I do not know what is in Your Self" is restricted to what is between him and his Lord and does not apply to everything. So he clarified by his saying: "Indeed You are the Knower of all that is Unseen" (عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ) — *the One possessing absolute and exhaustive knowledge of all that lies beyond sensory perception* — that perfect knowledge of all unseen things is confined to Him. Whatever is with any of the things and is unseen to others is known to Allah, the Glorified, and He encompasses it.

The necessary consequence of this is that nothing among the things knows His Unseen, the Exalted, nor the unseen of others which He, the Exalted, knows, because it is a limited creature that does not exceed the bounds of its own self. Thus, He is the Knower of all unseen things, and nothing other than He, the Exalted, knows anything of the unseen, neither the whole nor the part.

Furthermore, if something of His Unseen, the Exalted, were encompassed—if He, the Exalted, encompassed it, then this "encompasser" would not be an encompasser in reality, but rather encompassed by Him, the Exalted, whom Allah enabled by His will to encompass something of His kingdom without thereby exiting His kingdom, as the Exalted said: **وَلا يُحِيطُونَ بِشَيْءٍ مِنْ عِلْمِهِ إِلَّا بِما شاءَ** "and they do not comprehend anything of His knowledge except what He wishes" (2:255).

And if He, the Glorified, did not encompass what that thing encompassed, He would be struck by a limit and would thus be a creature—Exalted is Allah above that with great sublimity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ما قُلْتُ لَهُمْ إِلَّا ما أَمَرْتَنِي بِهِ أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ** "I did not say to them [anything] except what You had commanded me [to say]: ‘‘Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord’’" (5:117). When he (peace be upon him) denied the questioned saying from himself by denying its cause first, he denied it by clarifying his duty, which he did not exceed, second, saying: "I did not say to them except what You had commanded me, etc." He used restriction (الحصر) by way of negation and affirmation to indicate the answer by denying what he was asked about, which was the saying: "Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah."

He interpreted what his Lord commanded him to say by his saying: "Worship Allah," then he described Allah, the Glorified, by his saying: "my Lord and your Lord," so that not the slightest trace of illusion remains that he is anything but a messenger-servant calling to Allah, his Lord and the Lord of all people, alone without partner.

Upon this explicitness did Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him) proceed in his mission, calling them to monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — according to what the Noble Quran relates of him. The Exalted said, relating from him: **إِنَّ اللهَ هُوَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ** "Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path" (43:64), and He said: **وَإِنَّ اللهَ رَبِّي وَرَبُّكُمْ فَاعْبُدُوهُ هذا صِراطٌ مُسْتَقِيمٌ** "Indeed Allah is my Lord and your Lord; so worship Him. This is a straight path" (32:36).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكُنْتُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً ما دُمْتُ فِيهِمْ فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ وَأَنْتَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ شَهِيدٌ** "I was a witness to them so long as I was among them. But when You had me taken up, You were Yourself the watcher over them, and You are witness to all things" (5:117). Then he (peace be upon him) mentioned his second duty from the side of Allah, the Glorified, which is bearing witness (الشهادة) over the deeds of his community, as the Exalted said: **وَيَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يَكُونُ عَلَيْهِمْ شَهِيداً** "and on the Day of Resurrection he will be a witness against them" (4:159).

He (peace be upon him) says: I had no duty among them except the message to them and the witnessing over their deeds. As for the message, I delivered it in the most explicit way possible. As for the witnessing, I was engaged in it as long as I was among them, and I did not exceed the duty prescribed for me. Thus, I am innocent of having suggested to them to take me and my mother as two gods besides Allah.

His saying: **فَلَمَّا تَوَفَّيْتَنِي كُنْتَ أَنْتَ الرَّقِيبَ عَلَيْهِمْ** — "watching" (الرقوب) and "watchfulness" (الرقابة) is preservation (الحفظ), and what is meant by it in this context, by the indication of the flow of speech, is the preservation of deeds. It is as if he substituted "Witness" (الشهيد) with "Watcher" (الرقيب) to avoid repeating the word, considering his subsequent saying: "and You are witness to all things," and there is no specific point requiring the use of the word "Witness" again in particular.

The phrase, namely his saying: "You were Yourself the watcher over them," indicates restriction (الحصر), and its necessary consequence is that He, the Exalted, was a witness as long as Jesus (peace be upon him) was a witness, and a witness after him. Thus, his witnessing (peace be upon him) was a mediation in witnessing, not an independent witnessing, similar to all other divine managements over which He has appointed some of His servants, while He is the Trustee (وكيل) over everything, such as providing sustenance, giving life, causing death, preservation, calling, guidance, and others. The noble verses in this regard are many; there is no need to cite them.

Therefore, he (peace be upon him) followed his saying: "But when You had me taken up, You were Yourself the watcher over them" with his saying: "and You are witness to all things," to indicate thereby that the witnessing over the deeds of his community which he undertook as long as he was among them was a small portion of the general, absolute witnessing which is the witnessing of Allah, the Glorified, over a thing. For He, the Exalted, is a witness over the essences of things and over their actions, among which are the deeds of His servants, including the deeds of the community of Jesus as long as he was among them and after his being taken up. He, the Exalted, is a witness with the witnesses and a witness without them.

From here it becomes clear that the restriction is true in His right, the Exalted, despite the witnesses standing by their witnessing. For he (peace be upon him) restricted the witnessing after his being taken up to Allah, the Glorified, even though Allah has witnesses after him from among His servants and messengers, and he (peace be upon him) knows that.

Evidence for this is his glad tidings (peace be upon him) of the coming of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—according to what the Quran relates—by his saying: **يا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ مُصَدِّقاً لِما بَيْنَ يَدَيَّ مِنَ التَّوْراةِ وَمُبَشِّراً بِرَسُولٍ يَأْتِي مِنْ بَعْدِي اسْمُهُ أَحْمَدُ** "O Children of Israel! Indeed I am the apostle of Allah to you, confirming what is before me of the Torah, and giving the good news of an apostle who will come after me, whose name is Ahmad" (61:6). The Quran has explicitly stated that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) is among the witnesses, as the Exalted said: **وَجِئْنا بِكَ عَلى هؤُلاءِ شَهِيداً** "and bring you as a witness against them" (4:41).

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, related from him this restriction: "But when You had me taken up, You were Yourself the watcher over them," and He did not reject it as false. So Allah, the Glorified, is the Witness and none other, despite the existence of every witness; that is, the reality of witnessing belongs to Allah, the Glorified, just as the reality of every perfection and good belongs to Allah, the Glorified. Whatever perfection, good, or beauty another possesses is only by His granting of ownership, the Exalted, without this granting necessitating His removal from ownership, nor the cessation or nullification of His ownership. You must reflect on the various aspects of what we have mentioned.

Thus, it has become clear through the clarification he provided of his state, related in the two verses, that he is innocent of what the people said concerning him, and that there is no responsibility upon him for what they did. Therefore, he (peace be upon him) concluded his speech by saying: **إِنْ تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبادُكَ** "If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants" (5:118) to the end of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنْ تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبادُكَ وَإِنْ تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَإِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** "If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants; but if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise" (5:118). When it became clear through the proof he (peace be upon him) established that he had no duty toward the people except delivering the message and undertaking the matter of witnessing, and that he was occupied among them only with that and did not exceed it to what was not his right, he is not responsible for the word of disbelief they uttered. It became clear that he (peace be upon him) is separate from the divine judgment concerning them in what is between them and their Lord. Therefore, he resumed the speech again, saying without connection or derivation: "If You punish them, etc."

The verse is as if it were suitable to be placed in the position of the previous clarification. Its substance is: I have no responsibility for the hideous association (الشرك) they fell into, and I did not interfere in their affair in any way such that I would share with them in the judgment between You and them as You wish. They are subject to Your judgment concerning them as You desire, and they are subject to Your action among them as You have done. If You punish them by what You have judged for whoever associates partners with You by entering the Fire, they are indeed Your servants, and the management of their affair is Yours. You have the right to be displeased with them for it because You are the True Master (المولى الحق), and to the Master belongs the affair of His servants. And if You forgive them by erasing the trace of this great injustice (الظلم), then You are indeed the All-mighty (العزيز), the All-wise (الحكيم). To You belongs the right of Might and Wisdom. To the All-mighty—who is the One possessing such abundance and power as no other has—especially if He is All-wise—who does not undertake an affair unless it is something that ought to be undertaken—belongs the right to forgive the great injustice. For when Might and Wisdom embrace in an agent, they leave no power to stand against Him, nor any point of criticism in what He has decreed of an affair.

From the preceding clarification, it has become clear, firstly: that his saying: "they are indeed Your servants" is in the position of saying: "for You are their True Master," according to the custom of the Quran in mentioning the names of Allah after mentioning His acts, as at the end of the verse.

Secondly: that his saying: "You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise" is not intended for restriction (الحصر), but rather the use of the separating pronoun (ضمير الفصل) and the inclusion of the "L" (اللام) in the predicate is for emphasis. Its meaning boils down to the fact that Your Might and Your Wisdom are such that no doubt enters them; thus, there is no room for objection against You if You forgive them.

Thirdly: that the context—the context of direct address between Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him) and his Lord—since it was a context of the manifestation of divine Greatness before which nothing can stand, its requirement was to observe the aspect of the extreme lowliness of servitude by guarding against boldness and overstepping, and avoiding interference in the matter by supplication or request. Therefore, he (peace be upon him) said: "but if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise," and he did not say "for You are the All-forgiving, the All-merciful." This is because the radiance of the sign of divine Greatness and overwhelming Power that prevails over everything leaves the servant with no choice but to take refuge in Him with his lowliness of servitude and the poverty of absolute slavery and ownership. Overstepping at that moment is a great sin.

As for the saying of Abraham (peace be upon him) to his Lord: **فَمَنْ تَبِعَنِي فَإِنَّهُ مِنِّي وَمَنْ عَصانِي فَإِنَّكَ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** "So whoever follows me indeed belongs to me, and as for him who disobeys me, You are indeed All-forgiving, All-merciful" (14:36), it belongs to the context of supplication (الدعاء), and the servant has the right therein to stir the emergence of divine mercy as much as he can.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قالَ اللهُ هذا يَوْمُ يَنْفَعُ الصَّادِقِينَ صِدْقُهُمْ** "Allah will say, ‘This is a day when the truthful will benefit from their truthfulness’" (5:119). This is a confirmation of the truthfulness of Jesus son of Mary (peace be upon him) by way of allusion (التكنية), for He did not explicitly name him, but the context is what provides that.

What is meant by this truthfulness of the truthful is their truthfulness in the world, for He, the Exalted, follows this sentence with His saying: **لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ** "For them shall be gardens with conduits running amidst them" (5:119), etc. It is clear that this is a clarification of the reward for their truthfulness with Allah, the Glorified; so it is the benefit that returns to them from the aspect of truthfulness. Actions and states of the Hereafter—including the truthfulness of the people of the Hereafter—do not result in the effect of benefit in the sense of reward. In other words: actions and states of the Hereafter do not result in reward as do the actions and states of the world; for there is no religious obligation (تكليف) in the Hereafter, and reward is a branch of obligation. Rather, the Hereafter is the abode of reckoning and reward, just as the world is the abode of action and obligation. The Exalted said: **يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسابُ** "on the day when the reckoning will be held" (14:41), and He said: **الْيَوْمَ تُجْزَوْنَ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** "Today you will be requited for what you used to do" (45:28), and the Exalted said: **إِنَّما هذِهِ الْحَياةُ الدُّنْيا مَتاعٌ وَإِنَّ الْآخِرَةَ هِيَ دارُ الْقَرارِ** "This life of the world is only a passing enjoyment, and the Hereafter is indeed the abiding abode" (40:39).

What Jesus (peace be upon him) mentioned of his state in the world includes both word and deed, and Allah has confirmed him in truthfulness. Thus, the truthfulness mentioned in the verse includes truthfulness in deed just as it includes truthfulness in word. So the truthful in the world in their word and deed will benefit on the Day of Resurrection from their truthfulness; for them are the promised gardens, and they are the well-pleased and well-pleasing, successful with the great success.

Furthermore, truthfulness in word necessitates truthfulness in deed—in the sense of sincerity and the purification of action from the mark of hypocrisy—and leads to righteousness (الصلاح). It has been narrated that a man from the desert dwellers sought counsel from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and he counseled him not to lie. Then the man mentioned that observing what he was counseled with restrained him from all sins; for no sin presented itself except that he remembered that if he committed it and was then asked about it, he would be obliged to confess it against himself and inform the people, so he did not commit it for fear of that.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَهُمْ جَنَّاتٌ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها أَبَداً رَضِيَ اللهُ عَنْهُمْ وَرَضُوا عَنْهُ ذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ** "For them shall be gardens with conduits running amidst them, to remain in them forever. Allah is pleased with them and they are pleased with Him. That is the great triumph" (5:119). Allah is pleased with them for the truthfulness they offered Him, and they are pleased with Allah for the reward He gave them.

He has linked His pleasure to them themselves, not to their actions, as in His saying, the Exalted: **وَرَضِيَ لَهُ قَوْلاً** "and whose speech He approves" (20:109) and His saying: **وَإِنَّ تَشْكُرُوا يَرْضَهُ لَكُمْ** "and if you are grateful, He will approve it for you" (39:7). Between the two types of pleasure there is a difference; for your pleasure with something is that you do not reject it with dislike, and it is possible that your enemy performs an act you are pleased with while you are displeased with his person, and that your friend whom you love performs an act you are not pleased with.

So His saying: "Allah is pleased with them" indicates that Allah is pleased with their selves. It is known that pleasure does not relate to their selves unless His purpose—Glorified be His mention—in their creation is achieved. The Exalted has said: **وَما خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنْسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ** "I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me" (51:56). So servitude (العبودية) is the divine purpose for the creation of man. Allah, the Glorified, is only pleased with the self of His servant if he is an exemplar of servitude; that is, that his self be the self of a servant to Allah, who is the Lord of everything. Thus, he does not see himself or anything else except as owned by Allah, humble before His Lordship, returning only to his Lord and referring only to Him, as the Exalted said regarding Solomon and Job: **نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ** "What an excellent servant! Indeed he was a penitent [soul]" (38:44). This is the pleasure with him.

This is among the stations of servitude (مقامات العبودية), and its necessary consequence is the purification of the self from disbelief (الكفر) in its various degrees and from being characterized by transgression (الفسق), as the Exalted said: **وَلا يَرْضى لِعِبادِهِ الْكُفْرَ** "and He does not approve ungratefulness for His servants" (39:7) and the Exalted said: **فإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَرْضى عَنِ الْقَوْمِ الْفاسِقِينَ** "for Allah is not pleased with the transgressing lot" (9:96).

Among the effects of this station is that when servitude is established in the self of the servant and he sees whatever his eye falls upon and his insight reaches as owned by Allah and humble before His command, he becomes pleased with Allah. For he finds that everything Allah gave him, He only gave it from His grace without it being mandatory upon Him, so it is bounty and blessing; and that whatever He withheld, He only withheld it out of wisdom.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, mentions concerning them while they are in Paradise by His saying: **لَهُمْ فِيها ما يَشاؤُنَ** "They will have therein whatever they wish" (16:31, 25:16). It is known that when man finds everything he wishes, he cannot but be pleased.

This is the ultimate human happiness (السعادة الإنسانية) in his capacity as a servant. Therefore, He concluded the speech by saying: "That is the great triumph."

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لِلَّهِ مُلْكُ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَما فِيهِنَّ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** "To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is in them, and He has power over all things" (5:120). Ownership (المِلك) — with a *kasrah* — is a specific authority over the essence of things, and its effect is the penetration of the will in the disposal of them that the owner is capable of. Sovereignty (المُلك) — with a *dammah* — is a specific authority over the system existing between things, and its effect is the penetration of the will in what he is capable of. In simpler terms: ownership (المِلك) relates to the individual, and sovereignty (المُلك) relates to the group.

Since sovereignty in the actual penetration of the will is restricted by or constituted by power (القدرة), when power is complete and absolute, sovereignty is absolute sovereignty, not restricted by one thing over another or one state over another. To clarify this point, the Exalted followed His saying: "To Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth and whatever is in them" with His saying: "and He has power over all things."

The Surah concluded with this verse indicating absolute sovereignty, and the suitability is apparent. For the purpose of the Surah is to urge and encourage the servants to fulfill the covenants and pledges taken upon them from the side of their Lord, and He is the Sovereign in absolute terms. Thus, nothing remains for them except that they are servants owned in absolute terms, having nothing in what He commands them and forbids them except hearing and obedience, nor in what He takes from them of covenants and pledges except fulfilling them without breaking.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Ayyashi*, narrated from Tha'labah ibn Maymun from some of our companions from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, Blessed and Exalted, to Jesus: **أَأَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّي إِلهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ** — he said: "He has not said it yet, but he will say it. Indeed, when Allah knows that something will happen, He informs of it as if it has already happened."

I say: In it also, narrated from Sulayman ibn Khalid from Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): the same. Its gist is that the use of the past tense for a future matter is due to the certainty of its occurrence, and this is common in the language.

In it, narrated from Jabir al-Ju'fi from Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): regarding the interpretation of this verse: **تَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِي وَلا أَعْلَمُ ما فِي نَفْسِكَ ـ إِنَّكَ أَنْتَ عَلَّامُ الْغُيُوبِ** — he said: "Indeed, the Greatest Name of Allah (اسم الله الأكبر) consists of seventy-three letters. The Lord, Blessed and Exalted, veiled Himself from one of them; therefore, no one knows what is in His Self, Mighty and Majestic.

He gave Adam seventy-two letters, and the prophets inherited them—until they reached Jesus (peace be upon him). That is the saying of Jesus: 'You know what is in my self'—meaning the seventy-two letters of the Greatest Name—he says: 'You taught them to me, so You know them.' 'And I do not know what is in Your Self'—he says: 'Because You veiled Yourself with that letter—so no one knows what is in Your Self.'"

**I say:** The extensive discussion regarding the Beautiful Names of Allah and His Greatest Name (الاسم الأعظم الأكبر) will come in the commentary of His saying, the Exalted: **وَلِلَّهِ الْأَسْماءُ الْحُسْنى فَادْعُوهُ بِها** "To Allah belong the Best Names, so supplicate Him by them" (7:180). It will be clarified there that the Greatest Name or the Supreme Name is not of the type of word such that it would be composed of the letters of the alphabet. Rather, what is meant by the "Name" in such instances is that which is signified by the verbal name, which is the Essence (الذات) taken with one of its attributes and one of its aspects. The verbal name then becomes the "name of the name," as will be clarified later.

Accordingly, his saying (peace be upon him): "Indeed, the Greatest Name is composed of seventy-three letters," and similar narrations occurring in many reports in this chapter stating that the Greatest Name is composed of such-and-such letters, and that they are scattered and spread in such-and-such surahs or that it is in such-and-such verses—all of these are explanations based on symbolism (الرمز) and parables struck to convey what can be conveyed of the realities. For not every reality is easily clarified explicitly without metaphor, or by the thing itself rather than a parable.

What clarifies the meaning of the tradition to some extent is to say: there is no doubt that the Most Beautiful Names (الأَسْمَاءُ الْحُسْنَى) of Allah, the Exalted, are intermediaries for the manifestation of the cosmos in its specific entities and the occurrence of its countless events. For we do not doubt that Allah, the Glorified, created His creation because He is, for example, the Creator (الْخَالِقُ), the Generous (الْجَوَادُ), and the Originator (الْمُبْدِئُ), not because He is the Avenger (الْمُنْتَقِمُ) or the Severe in Punishment (شَدِيدُ الْبَطْشِ). He only provides for those He provides for because He is, for example, the Provider (الرَّازِقُ) and the Giver (الْمُعْطِي), not because He is the Withholder (الْقَابِضُ) or the Preventer (الْمَانِعُ). He only pours forth life upon the living because He is the Living (الْحَيُّ) and the Giver of Life (الْمُحْيِي), not because He is the Bringer of Death (الْمُمِيتُ) or the Restorer (الْمُعِيدُ). The Quranic verses are the truest witness to this reality; for we see the knowledge expounded in the core of the verses being justified by the Names appropriate to their meanings at their conclusion. Sometimes a verse concludes with one Name to clarify the meaning it contains, and sometimes it concludes with two Names which, in their combination, convey the mentioned meaning.

From here it becomes apparent that if one of us were granted the knowledge of the Names and the knowledge of the links between them and things, and what His Names, the Exalted, necessitate individually and in combination, he would know the cosmic system—both what has passed and what is occurring—through universal laws (قَوَانِينُ كُلِّيَّةٌ) applicable to their particulars (جُزْئِيَّاتِهَا) one by one.

The Noble Quran has explained, according to what is understood from its apparent meanings, many general laws regarding the Origin (الْمَبْدَأُ) and the Return (الْمَعَادُ), and what Allah, the Exalted, has ordained regarding the matter of felicity (السَّعَادَةُ) and wretchedness (الشَّقَاوَةُ). Then He addressed the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying: **وَنَزَّلْنَا عَلَيْكَ الْكِتَابَ تِبْيَانًا لِّكُلِّ شَيْءٍ** — "We have sent down the Book to you as a clarification of all things" (16:89).

However, all of these are necessary universal laws (قَوَانِينُ كُلِّيَّةٌ ضَرُورِيَّةٌ), yet they are necessary not in themselves or by the requirement of their own essences, but rather by the necessity and obligation that Allah, the Glorified, has bestowed upon them. Since this definitive rational governance (الْحُكُومَةُ الْعَقْلِيَّةُ) is from His side, the Exalted, and by His command and will, it is clear that His act, the Exalted, does not compel Him toward its own outcome, nor does it overcome Him in His essence. For He, the Glorified, is the Subduer (الْقَاهِرُ) and the Victorious (الْغَالِبُ); so how could He be overcome by that which traces back to Him from every direction and is impoverished toward Him in its very being and effect? Understand this.

It is impossible for the intellect (الْعَقْلُ)—which only judges what it judges by the effusion (إِفَاضَةُ) of Allah upon it—or for the realities (الْحَقَائِقُ)—whose judgments and effects only exist through Him, the Exalted—to be judges over Him, the Exalted, or to necessitate anything in Him through judgment or requirement, when He is the One who sustains both and is the Subduer and Victorious over them. In other words: whatever requirement (اقْتِضَاءٌ) or judgment (حُكْمٌ) exists in things is merely the effect of the ownership (التَّمْلِيكُ) that Allah has granted them. It is meaningless for a thing to possess, by the very ownership that Allah granted it, any power over Allah Himself. For He, the Exalted, is the Absolute Owner (مَالِكٌ عَلَى الْإِطْلَاقِ), not owned in any respect whatsoever.

Thus, if Allah were to reward the criminal or punish the doer of good, or perform any act He willed, there would be no harm upon Him, nor would any preventer—whether from the intellect or from without—stop Him. However, He, the Exalted, has promised us felicity and threatened us with wretchedness, good reward and evil punishment; and He has informed us that He does not fail in His promise. He has informed us through revelation (الْوَحْيُ) or the intellect (الْعَقْلُ) of certain matters, then mentioned that He says nothing but the truth. Thus, our souls found rest in Him and our hearts became tranquil toward Him with a certainty that leaves no path for doubt. The Exalted said: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ الْمِيعَادَ** — "Indeed Allah does not break His promise" (3:9, 13:31). And the Exalted said: **وَالْحَقَّ أَقُولُ** — "and I speak the truth" (38:84). Within the meaning of these lies the rational necessity (الضَّرُورَةُ الْعَقْلِيَّةُ) in its judgments.

This which He has explained is the requirement of His Names, the Exalted, among those He has taught us. Yet beyond that, He, the Exalted, is the Absolute Owner; He has the right to do what He wills and decree what He intends. The Exalted said: **لَا يُسْأَلُ عَمَّا يَفْعَلُ وَهُمْ يُسْأَلُونَ** — "He is not questioned about what He does, but they are questioned" (21:23). This very meaning is one of His Names, the Exalted, whose essence (الْكُنْهُ) is unknown; there is no way for the knowledge of any of His creation to attach to it. For everything we know of His Names is that which is reflected by a concept (مَفْهُومٌ), through which we then identify its effects in existence. As for the effects for which there is no way to identify them in existence, they are inevitably the effects of a Name for which there is no way to grasp its meaning. If you wish, you may say: it is a Name that cannot be captured by a concept, and the attribute of His Absolute Ownership (مُلْكُهُ الْمُطْلَقُ) only points to it with a kind of indication.

Thus, it has become clear that among His Names, the Exalted, is that to which no one of His creation has access, and it is that by which He has veiled Himself. Understand this.

## (Commentary on the Meaning of Adab)

In this section, we investigate the *adab* (الأَدَبُ) — *the refined manner of conduct* — with which He disciplined His prophets and messengers (peace be upon them), across several chapters:

**1 - [The Meaning of Adab]** — *Adab*, as derived from its meaning, is the beautiful form (الْهَيْئَةُ الْحَسَنَةُ) that a prescribed act should take, whether in religion or according to the rational people in their society, such as the *adab* of supplication or the *adab* of meeting friends. If you wish, you could say: the elegance of the act (ظَرَافَةُ الْفِعْلِ).

It only exists in prescribed matters that are not prohibited; thus, there is no *adab* in injustice, treachery, or lying, nor is there *adab* in heinous and ugly deeds. Furthermore, it is only realized in voluntary acts (الْأَفْعَالُ الِاخْتِيَارِيَّةُ) that have various forms, such that some are clothed in *adab* while others are not—such as the *adab* of eating in Islam, which is to begin with the Name of Allah, end with the praise of Allah, and eat without reaching satiety, among other things. Or the *adab* of sitting in prayer, which is to sit on the left thigh (*al-tawarruk*) with tranquility, placing the palms on the thighs above the knees, and looking toward one's lap, and so forth.

Since *adab* is the beautiful form in voluntary acts, and beauty (الْحُسْنُ)—though in its primary meaning it is the conformity to the purpose of life, a matter in which the views of societies do not differ—yet in terms of its instances (مَصَادِيقُهُ), it is a matter where the greatest disagreement occurs. According to the differences of peoples, nations, religions, and sects, and even small societies like the household and others, in identifying beauty and ugliness, differences arise among them regarding the *adab* of actions.

Perhaps one people possesses a form of *adab* unknown to others, or some *adab* considered beautiful by one people is seen as heinous and blameworthy by others—such as the greeting at the first meeting. In Islam, it is through the *salam* (التَّسْلِيمُ), a blessed and goodly greeting from Allah. For some peoples, it is by lifting hats; for some, by raising the hand toward the head; for others, by a prostration, a bowing, or an inclination by lowering the head. Similarly, in the *adab* of meeting women among Westerners, there are matters that Islam finds heinous and censures, and so on.

However, all these differences only arose at the stage of identifying the instance (الْمِصْدَاقُ). As for the fundamental meaning of *adab*—which is the beautiful form that an act ought to take—it is something upon which the rational among humanity are in total agreement, just as they are in total agreement on its goodness; no two people differ on this.

**2 - [The Diversity of Adab]** — Since beauty (الْحُسْنُ) is one of the constitutive elements of the meaning of *adab*, as mentioned in the previous chapter, and since it differs according to the specific objectives in different societies, this necessarily resulted in the diversity of human social *adab*. The *adab* in every society is like a mirror reflecting the general characteristics of that society's character (أَخْلَاقُ), which were arranged in them by their objectives in life and solidified in their souls by the factors of their social gathering and other various natural or coincidental factors.

*Adab* is not the same as character (الأَخْلَاقُ) — *the deep-seated spiritual traits (الْمَلَكَاتُ)* — because character refers to the firmly rooted spiritual dispositions with which souls are clothed. However, *adab* refers to the various beautiful forms with which the actions proceeding from a human being are clothed, arising from different psychological traits. There is a vast distance between the two.

Thus, *adab* is among the products of character, and character is among the requirements of a specific society according to its particular goal. The goal sought by a human being in his life is what identifies his *adab* in his actions and draws for his soul a line he does not cross when he performs an act in the course of his life and in drawing near to his goal.

**3 - [The Meaning of Divine Adab]** — Since *adab* follows the sought goal of life in its specificity, the Divine *adab* (الأَدَبُ الْإِلَهِيُّ) with which Allah, the Glorified, disciplined His prophets and messengers (peace be upon them) is the beautiful form in religious acts that reflects the purpose and goal of religion. That goal is servitude (الْعُبُودِيَّةُ) — *the state of total submission and devotion to Allah* — across the various true religions according to the abundance or scarcity of their material and according to their levels of perfection and advancement.

Since Islam is characterized by addressing all aspects of human life such that nothing of its affairs—whether minor or major, subtle or grand—escapes it, it has therefore encompassed life with *adab* and prescribed a beautiful form for every action that reflects its goal.

It has no general goal except the monotheism (التَّوْحِيدُ) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — of Allah, the Glorified, in both the stages of belief and action. That is, that the human being believes he has a God from Whom everything began and to Whom everything returns; to Him belong the Most Beautiful Names and the Highest Parables. Then he proceeds in life and lives with actions that themselves reflect his servitude and the servitude of everything he possesses to the True God, exalted is His Name (عز اسمه). By this, monotheism permeates his inner and outer being, and pure servitude (الْعُبُودِيَّةُ الْمَحْضَةُ) appears from his words, deeds, and all aspects of his existence—a manifestation with no cover over it and no veil to hide it.

Thus, Divine *adab*—or the *adab* of Prophethood—is the form of monotheism (هَيْئَةُ التَّوْحِيدِ) in action.

**4 - [Adab Only Yields Results with Action]** — It is known by reasoning and supported by definitive experience that the practical sciences (الْعُلُومُ الْعَمَلِيَّةُ)—which are learned in order to be acted upon—do not succeed fully nor produce their beautiful effect unless they are imparted to the learner within the context of action. This is because scientific universals (الْكُلِّيَّاتُ الْعِلْمِيَّةُ), as long as they are not applied to their particulars and instances, the soul is sluggish in confirming them and believing in their correctness. This is due to our souls being occupied throughout life with sensory particulars and their natural fatigue from observing rational universals that lie outside the senses. One who confirms the goodness of courage in itself according to a view devoid of action, then encounters one of the terrifying situations where hearts take flight, will find this leading to a conflict between his intellect—which judges the goodness of courage—and his imagination (الْوَهْمُ)—which pulls toward the pleasure of avoiding exposure to physical destruction and the loss of a comfortable material life. The soul continues to waver between this and that, bewildered in supporting one of the two contending sides, while the power is on the side of the imagination because the senses are with it.

Therefore, it is necessary during instruction that the learner receives scientific realities accompanied by action, so that he becomes trained and practiced through action. By this, the contrary beliefs residing in the corners of his soul vanish, and the confirmation of what he has learned becomes rooted in the soul, because occurrence is the best witness to possibility.

For this reason, we see that an action whose occurrence in the external world the soul is not accustomed to is difficult for it to submit to. When it occurs for the first time, it appears as if it has turned from impossibility to possibility; its occurrence seems momentous and causes anxiety and agitation in the soul. Then, when it occurs a second and third time, it becomes easy, its intensity breaks, and it joins the habitual things that are not given much weight. Indeed, good is a habit, just as evil is a habit.

Observing this method in religious instructions, especially in Islamic religious instruction, is among the clearest of matters. The Legislator of the religion never began teaching the believers with rational universals and general laws; rather, He began with action and accompanied it with speech and verbal explanation. Thus, when one of them completed learning the knowledge of religion and its laws, he completed it while equipped with righteous deeds and provided with the provision of godwariness (التَّقْوَى).

Similarly, it is necessary for the teacher and mentor to be a practitioner of his knowledge; for there is no effect in knowledge if it is not coupled with action. This is because action has a significance just as speech has a significance. An action contrary to speech indicates the presence of a contrary disposition in the soul that belies the speech; it indicates that the speech is a ruse and a kind of trickery by which the speaker schemes to deceive and trap people.

For this reason, we see that people's hearts do not soften nor do their souls submit to an exhortation or advice if they find the exhorter or the advisor not clothed in the action, turning away from patience and steadfastness in his path. They might say: "If what he said were true, he would have acted upon it." However, they might err in the conclusion they draw from this; for the correct conclusion is that the speech is not true *according to the speaker*, for had it been true in his view, he would have acted upon it. It does not conclude that the speech is not true *absolutely*, as they might conclude.

Thus, among the conditions of sound upbringing is that the teacher and mentor himself be characterized by what he describes to the learner, clothed in what he wishes to clothe him in. It is a habitual impossibility for a cowardly mentor to raise a brave and valiant person, or for a scholar who is free in his opinions and views to graduate from a school of prejudice and obstinacy, and so on.

The Exalted said: **أَفَمَن يَهْدِي إِلَى الْحَقِّ أَحَقُّ أَن يُتَّبَعَ أَمَّن لَّا يَهِدِّي إِلَّا أَن يُهْدَىٰ ۖ فَمَا لَكُمْ كَيْفَ تَحْكُمُونَ** — "Is He who guides to the truth worthier to be followed, or he who guides not unless he [himself] is guided? What is the matter with you? How do you judge?" (10:35). And He said: **أَتَأْمُرُونَ النَّاسَ بِالْبِرِّ وَتَنسَوْنَ أَنفُسَكُمْ** — "Will you bid others to piety and forget yourselves" (2:44). And He said, narrating the words of Shu’ayb to his people: **وَمَا أُرِيدُ أَنْ أُخَالِفَكُمْ إِلَىٰ مَا أَنْهَاكُمْ عَنْهُ ۚ إِنْ أُرِيدُ إِلَّا الْإِصْلَاحَ مَا اسْتَطَعْتُ** — "I do not desire to oppose you by [doing] what I forbid you. I desire nothing but reform as far as I can" (11:88), among other verses.

For all of this, it was necessary for the teacher and mentor to be a believer in the materials of his instruction and upbringing.

Furthermore, a person devoid of faith in what he says—even the hypocrite (الْمُنَافِقُ) who hides behind righteous deeds and feigns explicit, sincere faith—will only raise those who represent him in his own wicked soul. For although it is possible for the tongue to project a difference from the heart by speaking what the soul does not approve of and what the secret does not agree with, speech from another perspective is an action, and action is among the effects and emanations of the soul. How can an action oppose the nature of its doer?

Thus, speech—aside from the perspective of conventional linguistic indication—carries the nature of the speaker's soul, whether faith, disbelief, or otherwise, and places it and delivers it to the simple, naive soul of the learner. No one can distinguish the aspect of its goodness—which is the aspect of its conventional indication—from the aspect of its corruption—which is its other aspects—except one who possesses insight into the matter. The Exalted said, describing the hypocrites to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: **وَلَتَعْرِفَنَّهُمْ فِي لَحْنِ الْقَوْلِ** — "and you will surely recognize them by their tone of speech" (47:30). Thus, the upbringing that results in a righteous effect is that in which the teacher and mentor possesses faith in what he imparts to his students, accompanied by righteous deeds (الْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ) that conform to his knowledge. As for the one who does not believe in what he says or does not act according to his knowledge, no good is expected from him.

This reality has many instances and countless examples in our conduct as Easterners and Muslims, especially in education and upbringing in our formal and informal institutions; thus, hardly any management avails, nor does any effort succeed.

**5 - [The General Adab of Prophethood in Brief]** — To this category belongs what we see of His speech, the Exalted, containing the narration of chapters of Divine *adab* manifested in the actions of the prophets and messengers (peace be upon them)—whether regarding what pertains to Allah, the Glorified, such as their types of worship, supplications, and requests, or what pertains to people in their social interactions and addresses. For the presentation of examples in instruction is a type of practical instruction by witnessing the action.

Allah, the Exalted, said after mentioning the story of Ibrahim regarding monotheism with his people: **وَتِلْكَ حُجَّتُنَا آتَيْنَاهَا إِبْرَاهِيمَ عَلَىٰ قَوْمِهِ ۚ نَرْفَعُ دَرَجَاتٍ مَّن نَّشَاءُ ۗ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ حَكِيمٌ عَلِيمٌ * وَوَهَبْنَا لَهُ إِسْحَاقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ ۚ كُلًّا هَدَيْنَا ۚ وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِ دَاوُدَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُوسُفَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَهَارُونَ ۚ وَكَذَٰلِكَ نَجْزِي الْمُحْسِنِينَ * وَزَكَرِيَّا وَيَحْيَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَإِلْيَاسَ ۖ كُلٌّ مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ * وَإِسْمَاعِيلَ وَالْيَسَعَ وَيُونُسَ وَلُوطًا ۚ وَكُلًّا فَضَّلْنَا عَلَى الْعَالَمِينَ * وَمِنْ آبَائِهِمْ وَذُرِّيَّاتِهِمْ وَإِخْوَانِهِمْ ۖ وَاجْتَبَيْنَاهُمْ وَهَدَيْنَاهُمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ * ذَٰلِكَ هُدَى اللَّهِ يَهْدِي بِهِ مَن يَشَاءُ مِنْ عِبَادِهِ ۚ وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ * أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ آتَيْنَاهُمُ الْكِتَابَ وَالْحُكْمَ وَالنُّبُوَّةَ ۚ فَإِن يَكْفُرْ بِهَا هَٰؤُلَاءِ فَقَدْ وَكَّلْنَا بِهَا قَوْمًا لَّيْسُوا بِهَا بِكَافِرِينَ * أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللَّهُ ۖ فَبِهُدَاهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ** — "This was Our argument, which We gave to Abraham against his people. We raise in rank whomever We wish. Indeed your Lord is all-wise, all-knowing. And We gave him Isaac and Jacob and guided each of them. And Noah We had guided before, and from his offspring, David and Solomon, Job, Joseph, Moses and Aaron—thus do We reward the virtuous—and Zechariah, John, Jesus and Elias—each of them among the righteous—and Ishmael, Elisha, Jonah and Lot—each of them We exalted above all the nations—and from among their fathers, their offspring and their brothers—We chose them and guided them to a straight path. That is Allah’s guidance: with it He guides whomever He wishes of His servants. But were they to ascribe any partners [to Him], what they used to do would surely be null and void. They are the ones whom We gave the Book, judgement and prophethood. So if these disbelieve in them, We have certainly entrusted them to a people who will never disbelieve in them. They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance" (6:83-90). The Exalted mentions His noble prophets (peace be upon them) collectively, then mentions that He honored them with Divine guidance (الْهِدَايَةُ الْإِلَهِيَّةُ), which is guidance to monotheism alone. The proof for this is His saying: **وَلَوْ أَشْرَكُوا لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُمْ** — "But were they to ascribe any partners [to Him], what they used to do would surely be null and void." He mentioned nothing that contradicts what He bestowed upon them of guidance except polytheism (الشِّرْكُ); thus, He guided them only to monotheism.

However, monotheism is such that its judgment permeates their actions and is established within them. The proof for this is His saying: **لَحَبِطَ عَنْهُم مَّا كَانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "what they used to do would surely be null and void." For if polytheism did not run through actions and seep into them, it would not necessitate their nullification; thus, the monotheism that opposes it is the same.

The meaning of monotheism permeating actions is that their forms represent monotheism and reflect it as a mirror reflects what is seen in it, such that if it were assumed that monotheism took a form, it would be those very actions; and if those actions were stripped into pure belief, they would be monotheism itself.

This meaning has many instances in spiritual traits; for you see the actions of the arrogant person representing the trait of arrogance and conceit in his soul. Similarly, the movements and stillness of the wretched, poor person reflect the humiliation and submissiveness in his secret heart, and so on.

Then the Exalted disciplined His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, commanding him to follow the guidance of the prophets who preceded him (peace be upon them), not to follow them themselves. Following (الِاقْتِدَاءُ) only occurs in action, not in belief, for belief is not voluntary in itself. That is, he should choose their righteous deeds based on monotheism, proceeding from them through a practical Divine discipline.

By this practical discipline (التَّأْدِيبُ الْعَمَلِيُّ), we mean what is indicated by His saying, the Exalted: **وَجَعَلْنَاهُمْ أَئِمَّةً يَهْدُونَ بِأَمْرِنَا وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ وَإِقَامَ الصَّلَاةِ وَإِيتَاءَ الزَّكَاةِ ۖ وَكَانُوا لَنَا عَابِدِينَ** — "We made them imams, guiding by Our command, and We revealed to them the performance of good deeds, the maintenance of prayer, and the giving of zakat, and they used to be Our worshippers" (21:73). The annexation of the verbal noun in His saying **فِعْلَ الْخَيْرَاتِ** (the performance of good deeds) etc., indicates that what is intended is the action proceeding from them—of good deeds they performed, prayer they maintained, and zakat they gave—not merely a hypothetical action. This revelation (الْوَحْيُ) pertaining to actions at the stage of their proceeding from them is a revelation of guidance and discipline (وَحْيُ تَسْدِيدٍ وَتَأْدِيبٍ), not the revelation of prophethood and legislation (وَحْيُ النُّبُوَّةِ وَالتَّشْرِيعِ). Had the intention been the revelation of prophethood, it would have been said: "And We revealed to them *that they should* perform good deeds, maintain prayer, and give zakat," as in His saying, the Exalted: **ثُمَّ أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ أَنِ اتَّبِعْ** — "Then We revealed to you: ‘Follow...’" (16:123) and His saying: **وَأَوْحَيْنَا إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَأَخِيهِ أَن تَبَوَّآ لِقَوْمِكُمَا بِمِصْرَ بُيُوتًا وَاجْعَلُوا بُيُوتَكُمْ قِبْلَةً وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ** — "We revealed to Moses and his brother [saying], ‘Settle your people in city houses, and make your houses places of worship, and maintain the prayer’" (10:87), among other verses. The meaning of the revelation of guidance (وَحْيُ التَّسْدِيدِ) is that Allah singles out one of His servants with a holy spirit (رُوحٌ قُدُسِيٌّ) that guides him in good deeds and in avoiding evil, just as the human spirit guides us in thinking about good and evil, and the animal spirit guides us in choosing what we desire of attraction and repulsion by will. The detailed discussion on this will come, if Allah wills.

In short, His saying: **فَبِهُدَاهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ** — "So follow their guidance" — is a general Divine discipline for him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with the *adab* of monotheism spread over the actions of the prophets (peace be upon them), which are purified from polytheism.

Then the Exalted said—after mentioning several of His prophets (peace be upon them)—in Surah Maryam: **أُولَٰئِكَ الَّذِينَ أَنْعَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِم مِّنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِن ذُرِّيَّةِ آدَمَ وَمِمَّنْ حَمَلْنَا مَعَ نُوحٍ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّةِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَإِسْرَائِيلَ وَمِمَّنْ هَدَيْنَا وَاجْتَبَيْنَا ۚ إِذَا تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْهِمْ آيَاتُ الرَّحْمَنِ خَرُّوا سُجَّدًا وَبُكِيًّا * فَخَلَفَ مِن بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَوَاتِ ۖ فَسَوْفَ يَلْقَوْنَ غَيًّا * إِلَّا مَن تَابَ وَآمَنَ وَعَمِلَ صَالِحًا فَأُولَٰئِكَ يَدْخُلُونَ الْجَنَّةَ وَلَا يُظْلَمُونَ شَيْئًا** — "They are the ones whom Allah has blessed from among the prophets of Adam’s offspring, and of those We carried with Noah, and of Abraham’s offspring and Israel’s, and of those We guided and chose. When the signs of the All-beneficent were recited to them, they would fall down weeping in prostration. But they were succeeded by a generations who neglected the prayer and followed their appetites. So they will soon encounter leeway [in hell], barring those who repent, believe, and act righteously. Such will enter paradise, and they will not be wronged in the least" (19:58-60).

The Exalted mentioned their general *adab* in their lives: that they live upon submission (الْخُضُوعُ) in action and upon humility (الْخُشُوعُ) in heart for Allah, exalted is His Name. For their prostration upon the mention of the signs of Allah is an example of submission, and their weeping—which is due to the tenderness of the heart and the self-abasement of the soul—is a sign of humility. Both together are a metaphor for the dominance of the trait of servitude (الْعُبُودِيَّةُ) over their souls, such that whenever they are reminded by a sign from the signs of Allah, its effect appears in their outward being just as the trait has dominated their inward being. Thus, they are upon their Divine *adab*, which is the mark of servitude, when they are alone with their Lord and when they are alone with people; they live upon a Divine *adab* with their Lord and with all people.

Among the proofs that what is intended is the general *adab* is His saying, the Exalted, in the second verse: **فَخَلَفَ مِن بَعْدِهِمْ خَلْفٌ أَضَاعُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَاتَّبَعُوا الشَّهَوَاتِ** — "But they were succeeded by a generations who neglected the prayer and followed their appetites." For prayer—which is the turning toward Allah—is their state with their Lord, and following appetites is their state with others among people. Since those were contrasted with these, the speech implies that the general *adab* of the prophets is to refer to their Lord with the mark of servitude and to walk among people with the mark of servitude. That is, the structure of their lives is built on the foundation that they have a Lord Who owns them and manages their affairs; from Him was their beginning and to Him is their return. This is the principle in all their states and actions.

What the Exalted mentioned regarding the exception of the repentant among them is another Divine *adab* that began with Adam (peace be upon him), the first of the prophets, where He said: **وَعَصَىٰ آدَمُ رَبَّهُ فَغَوَىٰ * ثُمَّ اجْتَبَاهُ رَبُّهُ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ** — "Adam disobeyed his Lord, and went amiss. Then his Lord chose him, and turned to him clemently, and guided him" (20:121-122). Some discussion on this will follow, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

The Exalted said: **مَّا كَانَ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ مِنْ حَرَجٍ فِيمَا فَرَضَ اللَّهُ لَهُ ۖ سُنَّةَ اللَّهِ فِي الَّذِينَ خَلَوْا مِن قَبْلُ ۚ وَكَانَ أَمْرُ اللَّهِ قَدَرًا مَّقْدُورًا * الَّذِينَ يُبَلِّغُونَ رِسَالَاتِ اللَّهِ وَيَخْشَوْنَهُ وَلَا يَخْشَوْنَ أَحَدًا إِلَّا اللَّهَ ۗ وَكَفَىٰ بِاللَّهِ حَسِيبًا** — "There is no blame on the Prophet in respect of what Allah has ordained for him: Allah’s custom with those who passed away before, and Allah’s command is a determinate decree. Such as deliver the messages of Allah and fear Him, and fear no one except Allah, and Allah suffices as a reckoner" (33:38-39).

This is a general *adab* with which Allah, the Glorified, disciplined His prophets (peace be upon them) and a custom of His that runs through them: that they should not feel constrained in what He has apportioned for them of life, nor should they burden themselves (يَتَكَلَّفُوا) in any matter. This is because they were upon the primordial nature (الْفِطْرَةُ) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and the primordial nature only guides to that for which Allah has equipped it with what suits it in attaining it. It does not burden itself to rise to what Allah has not made easy for it to ascend to its level. The Exalted said, narrating from His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: **وَمَا أَنَا مِنَ الْمُتَكَلِّفِينَ** — "and I am not one of the affected" (38:86). And the Exalted said: **لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا** — "Allah does not task any soul beyond its capacity" (2:286). And the Exalted said: **لَا يُكَلِّفُ اللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا مَا آتَاهَا** — "Allah does not task any soul except [according to] what He has given it" (65:7). Since self-burdening (التَّكَلُّفُ) is a departure from the primordial nature, it is part of following appetite, and the prophets are secure from it.

The Exalted said—and this is also part of the discipline with a comprehensive *adab*: **يَا أَيُّهَا الرُّسُلُ كُلُوا مِنَ الطَّيِّبَاتِ وَاعْمَلُوا صَالِحًا ۖ إِنِّي بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ عَلِيمٌ * وَإِنَّ هَٰذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً وَاحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاتَّقُونِ** — "O apostles! Eat of the good things and act righteously. Indeed I know best what you do. Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so be wary of Me" (23:51-52). He disciplined them to eat of the good things (الطَّيِّبَاتِ)—that is, to utilize the wholesome materials of life and not cross over to the foul things (الْخَبَائِثِ) from which the sound primordial nature recoils—and to perform righteous deeds (الْعَمَلُ الصَّالِحُ), which are those actions suitable for a human to perform, toward which the primordial nature inclines according to how Allah has equipped it with means that preserve its survival through its action until its time. Or, it means to perform the action that is suitable to be presented to the Presence of Lordship; the two meanings are close. This is an *adab* pertaining to the individual human being.

Then the Exalted connected it with a social *adab*, mentioning to them that people are but one community: the messengers and those to whom they were sent. They have but one Lord, so let them gather upon His godwariness (تَقْوَاهُ), and thereby cut off the root of differences and factions. When the two matters meet—I mean the individual and social *adab*—a single human society is formed, protected from difference, worshipping one Lord, and its individuals proceed upon the Divine *adab*; so be wary of foul deeds and evil actions, for they have then settled upon the throne of felicity.

This is what another verse has gathered, which is His saying, the Exalted: **شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ ۖ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it’" (42:13).

Allah has separated the two *adabs* in another place, saying: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا نُوحِي إِلَيْهِ أَنَّهُ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاعْبُدُونِ** — "We did not send any apostle before you but that We revealed to him that ‘There is no god except Me; so worship Me’" (21:25). Thus, He disciplined them with His monotheism and building worship upon it; this is their *adab* in relation to their Lord.
