Punishment and reward have a wide range, starting from dislike and approval, and blame and praise, to the end of what power attaches to of evil and good. They are linked in that to various factors of the specifics of the act, the actor, the guardian of the duty, and the amount of harm and benefit returning to the society. Perhaps what unites them all is that whenever the concern for the matter of the act increases, it increases in punishment in the case of disobedience and in reward in the case of obedience.

Between the act and its retribution—whatever it may be—a type of similarity (مماثلة) and correspondence (مسانخة) is considered, even if approximate. Upon this also runs His speech, the Exalted, as is apparent from the likes of His saying, the Exalted: **لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا بِمَا عَمِلُوا وَيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا بِالْحُسْنَى** — "that He may requite those who do evil for what they have done, and reward those who do good with the best [reward]" (53:31). And clearer than it is His saying, the Exalted, as He recounted from the scrolls of Abraham and Moses (peace be upon them): **وَأَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسَانِ إِلَّا مَا سَعَى * وَأَنَّ سَعْيَهُ سَوْفَ يُرَى * ثُمَّ يُجْزَاهُ الْجَزَاءَ الْأَوْفَى** — "and that nothing belongs to man except what he strives for, and that he will soon be shown his endeavour, then he will be rewarded for it with the fullest reward" (53:39-41).

This is most apparent in what Allah legislated regarding the matter of retaliation (القصاص); the Exalted said: **كُتِبَ عَلَيْكُمُ الْقِصَاصُ فِي الْقَتْلَى الْحُرُّ بِالْحُرِّ وَالْعَبْدُ بِالْعَبْدِ وَالْأُنْثَى بِالْأُنْثَى** — "Retribution is prescribed for you regarding the slain: the free for the free, the slave for the slave, and the female for the female" (2:178). And He said: **الشَّهْرُ الْحَرَامُ بِالشَّهْرِ الْحَرَامِ وَالْحُرُمَاتُ قِصَاصٌ فَمَنِ اعْتَدَى عَلَيْكُمْ فَاعْتَدُوا عَلَيْهِ بِمِثْلِ مَا اعْتَدَى عَلَيْكُمْ وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ** — "A sacred month for a sacred month, and all sanctities are [subject to] retribution. So should anyone aggress against you, assail him in the manner he assailed you, and be wary of Allah" (2:194).

The necessity of this similarity and correspondence is that the punishment or reward returns to the actor himself with the likeness of what he did, in the sense that if he disobeys a social ruling, for example, he has only enjoyed for himself what harms the society—that is, what spoils one of the enjoyments of the society—so there is decreased from his own enjoyments in himself what equals that from his self, his body, his wealth, his status, or the like of what returns in some way to him.

This is what we alluded to in the research on the meaning of enslavement: that the society or the one who manages its affairs possesses from the criminal his self or an aspect of the aspects of his self that equals the crime he committed and the deficiency of harm he inflicted upon the society. He is punished with that—meaning the society or the Guardian disposes, based on this ownership (which is the right), in the life of the criminal or an aspect of the aspects of his life, and strips his freedom in that.

If he kills a soul, for example, without [retaliation for] a soul or corruption in the earth in the Islamic society, the Guardian possesses from the criminal his self since he deprived them of a respected soul. His legal punishment (حد), which is killing, is a disposal in his self based on the ownership that he possessed. If he steals what reaches a quarter of a dinar from a secure place, he has harmed the society by violating one of the veils of public security which the hand of the Law drew and the hand of trust preserved. Its legal punishment, which is amputation, its reality is nothing but that the Guardian possessed from the thief, in exchange for what he did, an aspect of the aspects of his life—which is the aspect that the hand comprises—so he disposes in it by stripping what he has of freedom and its means from this perspective. Measure by this the types of retribution in different laws and customs.

It becomes clear from here that crime and social disobedience attract a type of slavery and servitude. Therefore, the slave (العبد) was the most apparent manifestation of accountability and punishment; the Exalted said: **إِنْ تُعَذِّبْهُمْ فَإِنَّهُمْ عِبَادُكَ** — "If You punish them, they are indeed Your servants" (5:118).

This meaning has scattered manifestations in other various laws and customs. Allah the Exalted said in the story of Joseph (peace be upon him) when he placed the drinking cup in his brother's saddlebag to take him to himself: **قَالُوا فَمَا جَزَاؤُهُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ كَاذِبِينَ** — "They said, ‘What shall be its requital if you are liars?’" (12:73)—meaning in your denial of stealing the king's goblet—**قَالُوا جَزَاؤُهُ مَنْ وُجِدَ فِي رَحْلِهِ فَهُوَ جَزَاؤُهُ كَذَلِكَ نَجْزِي الظَّالِمِينَ** — "They said, ‘Its requital is he in whose saddlebag it is found: he shall be its [own] requital. Thus do we requite the wrongdoers’" (12:75)—meaning we requite the thief by enslaving him—**فَبَدَأَ بِأَوْعِيَتِهِمْ قَبْلَ وِعَاءِ أَخِيهِ ثُمَّ اسْتَخْرَجَهَا مِنْ وِعَاءِ أَخِيهِ كَذَلِكَ كِدْنَا لِيُوسُفَ** — "Then he began with their sacks before the sack of his brother, then he took it out from his brother’s sack. Thus did We devise for Joseph" (12:76)—until He said—**قَالُوا يَا أَيُّهَا الْعَزِيزُ إِنَّ لَهُ أَبًا شَيْخًا كَبِيرًا فَخُذْ أَحَدَنَا مَكَانَهُ إِنَّا نَرَاكَ مِنَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "They said, ‘O Emir! He has a very aged father. So take one of us in his place. Indeed we see you to be a virtuous man’" (12:78)—and this is substitution and a type of ransom—**قَالَ مَعَاذَ اللَّهِ أَنْ نَأْخُذَ إِلَّا مَنْ وَجَدْنَا مَتَاعَنَا عِنْدَهُ إِنَّا إِذًا لَظَالِمُونَ** — "He said, ‘Allah forbid that we should take anyone except him with whom we found our goods, for then we would be wrongdoers’" (12:79).

Perhaps the killer would be taken as a captive slave, and perhaps he would be ransomed by one of his women and family, like his daughter or sister, among other things. The custom of ransom through marriage was practiced until these days among the tribes and clans in our regions because marriage is considered by them a type of enslavement and captivity for women.

From here, the obedient one is sometimes considered a slave to the one obeyed, because by his obedience he follows with his will the will of the one obeyed; thus he is his possession, deprived of freedom of will. The Exalted said: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يَا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لَا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطَانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ * وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي** — "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me’" (36:60-61). And He said: **أَفَرَأَيْتَ مَنِ اتَّخَذَ إِلَهَهُ هَوَاهُ** — "Have you seen him who has taken his desire to be his god" (45:23).

Conversely to the society or the Guardian possessing the punished criminal, the rewarded obedient one possesses from the society or the Guardian what balances his obedience of reward. For the society or the Guardian decreased from the obedient duty-bound one, by means of the duty, something of his gifted freedom, so it is upon him to complete it as it was decreased.

This which we have mentioned is the secret in what is famous: that fulfilling a promise (الوعد) is obligatory, unlike a threat (الوعيد). That is because the content of a promise in the context of mastership and servitude is reward for obedience, just as the content of a threat is punishment for disobedience. Reward, since it is a right for the obedient one over the Guardian and is a liability upon him, it is obligatory for him to fulfill it and discharge his liability from it, unlike punishment, for it is a right for the Guardian over the criminal duty-bound one, and it is not obligatory for a person to dispose in his property and benefit from his right if he has the choice to do so. There is a completion to this discussion.

**3 — Forgiveness and Pardon?**
We concluded from the previous research the permissibility of leaving retribution for disobedience, unlike obedience. This is a primordial judgment in general, based on the fact that punishment is a right for the one disobeyed over the disobedient, and it is not obligatory to exercise a right always.

However, just as it is not obligatory to exercise the right of punishment always, likewise it is not permissible to leave it always; otherwise, the primordial judgment of the establishment of the right would be nullified, and there is no meaning to the establishment of something that has no effect, even at some time. Furthermore, the nullification of the right of punishment from its root is a demolition of the established laws that preserve the structure of society, and in their demolition is the demolition of society without doubt.

So the ruling—which is the permissibility of forgiving a sin—is established in general, and the proposition is indefinite. If there is a justifying cause according to Divine Wisdom (الحكمة) for forgiveness, then forgiveness is permissible; otherwise, retribution is obligatory out of respect for the laws that preserve the structure of society and the happiness of man. To this is the allusion in His saying, the Exalted, recounting from Jesus (peace be upon him): **وَإِنْ تَغْفِرْ لَهُمْ فَإِنَّكَ أَنْتَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "and if You forgive them, You are indeed the All-mighty, the All-wise" (5:118).

There exist in the Noble Quran two universal causes for forgiveness that Divine Wisdom endorses:

One of them is the servant’s repentance (التوبة) toward Allah, the Glorified, which is broader than merely returning from disbelief to faith or from disobedience to obedience, as previously explained in the discussion on repentance in the fourth volume of this book. The Exalted said: **قُلْ يَا عِبَادِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسْرَفُوا عَلَىٰ أَنفُسِهِمْ لَا تَقْنَطُوا مِن رَّحْمَةِ اللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَغْفِرُ الذُّنُوبَ جَمِيعًا ۚ إِنَّهُ هُوَ الْغَفُورُ الرَّحِيمُ * وَأَنِيبُوا إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُمْ وَأَسْلِمُوا لَهُ مِن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذَابُ ثُمَّ لَا تُنصَرُونَ** — "Say, [from Allah]: 'O My servants who have committed excesses against their own souls, do not despair of the mercy of Allah. Indeed Allah forgives all sins. Indeed He is the All-forgiving, the All-merciful. And turn confidingly to your Lord, and submit to Him before the punishment overtakes you, for then you will not be helped.'" (39:53-54). This repentance is from the disbelief (الكفر) that carries the threat of punishment, wherein no helper or intercessor avails. Then He said: **وَاتَّبِعُوا أَحْسَنَ مَا أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُم مِّن قَبْلِ أَن يَأْتِيَكُمُ الْعَذَابُ بَغْتَةً وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ** — "And follow the best of what has been sent down to you from your Lord, before the punishment overtakes you suddenly while you are unaware." (39:55). This refers to repentance from disobedience (المعصية) to obedience (الطاعة), and in this case, He did not negate the benefit of intercession.

The Exalted also said: **إِنَّمَا التَّوْبَةُ عَلَى اللَّهِ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السُّوءَ بِجَهَالَةٍ ثُمَّ يَتُوبُونَ مِن قَرِيبٍ فَأُولَٰئِكَ يَتُوبُ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۗ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَلِيمًا حَكِيمًا * وَلَيْسَتِ التَّوْبَةُ لِلَّذِينَ يَعْمَلُونَ السَّيِّئَاتِ حَتَّىٰ إِذَا حَضَرَ أَحَدَهُمُ الْمَوْتُ قَالَ إِنِّي تُبْتُ الْآنَ وَلَا الَّذِينَ يَمُوتُونَ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ ۚ أُولَٰئِكَ أَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابًا أَلِيمًا** — "Repentance [accepted] by Allah is only for those who commit evil out of ignorance and then repent promptly. It is such whose repentance Allah shall accept, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. But repentance is not [accepted] from those who go on committing misdeeds until, when death visits one of them, he says, ‘I repent now,’ nor from those who die while they are faithless. For such We have prepared a painful punishment." (4:17-18).

The second is intercession (الشفاعة) — *the act of mediating on behalf of others before Allah* — on the Day of Resurrection. The Exalted said: **وَلَا يَمْلِكُ الَّذِينَ يَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِ الشَّفَاعَةَ إِلَّا مَن شَهِدَ بِالْحَقِّ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ** — "Those whom they invoke besides Him have no power of intercession, except those who are witness to the truth and who know [it]." (43:86), among many other verses addressing the matter of intercession. We have extensively discussed this in the section on intercession in the first volume of this book.

There are scattered instances in the Noble Quran where pardon (العفو) is mentioned without specifying its cause, although contemplation (التدبر) leads to a general understanding of the underlying wisdom (المصلحة) observed therein, which is the interest of the religion (الدين). Examples include His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ عَفَا عَنكُمْ ۗ وَاللَّهُ ذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "He has certainly excused you, and Allah is gracious to the faithful." (3:152); and His saying, the Exalted: **أَأَشْفَقْتُمْ أَن تُقَدِّمُوا بَيْنَ يَدَيْ نَجْوَاكُمْ صَدَقَاتٍ ۚ فَإِذْ لَمْ تَفْعَلُوا وَتَابَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْكُمْ فَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَآتُوا الزَّكَاةَ وَأَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ** — "Were you afraid to offer charities before your confidential talks? Since you did not do it and Allah has forgiven you, maintain the prayer and pay the zakat, and obey Allah and His Apostle." (58:13); and His saying, the Exalted: **لَّقَد تَّابَ اللَّهُ عَلَى النَّبِيِّ وَالْمُهَاجِرِينَ وَالْأَنصَارِ الَّذِينَ اتَّبَعُوهُ فِي سَاعَةِ الْعُسْرَةِ مِن بَعْدِ مَا كَادَ يَزِيغُ قُلُوبُ فَرِيقٍ مِّنْهُمْ ثُمَّ تَابَ عَلَيْهِمْ ۚ إِنَّهُ بِهِمْ رَءُوفٌ رَّحِيمٌ** — "Allah has certainly turned [mercifully] to the Prophet and the Emigrants and the Helpers who followed him in the hour of difficulty, after the hearts of a part of them had almost swerved. Then He turned [mercifully] to them. Indeed He is most kind and merciful to them." (9:117); and His saying, the Exalted: **وَحَسِبُوا أَلَّا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ فَعَمُوا وَصَمُّوا ثُمَّ تَابَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ عَمُوا وَصَمُّوا كَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ** — "They supposed there would be no testing, so they became blind and deaf. Then Allah turned to them [mercifully], yet [again] many of them became blind and deaf." (5:71); and His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ يُظَاهِرُونَ مِنكُم مِّن نِّسَائِهِم مَّا هُنَّ أُمَّهَاتِهِمْ ۖ إِنْ أُمَّهَاتُهُمْ إِلَّا اللَّائِي وَلَدْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّهُمْ لَيَقُولُونَ مُنكَرًا مِّنَ الْقَوْلِ وَزُورًا ۚ وَإِنَّ اللَّهَ لَعَفُوٌّ غَفُورٌ** — "As for those of you who repudiate their wives by zihar, they are not their mothers; their mothers are only those who gave birth to them. Indeed they utter an innerly [sic] and monstrous lie. Yet Allah is surely all-excusing, all-forgiving." (58:2); and His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لَا تَقْتُلُوا الصَّيْدَ وَأَنتُمْ حُرُمٌ... عَفَا اللَّهُ عَمَّا سَلَفَ ۚ وَمَنْ عَادَ فَيَنْتَقِمُ اللَّهُ مِنْهُ ۗ وَاللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو انتِقَامٍ** — "O you who have faith! Do not kill any game while you are in pilgrim sanctity... Allah has dispensed with what is past; but whoever resumes, Allah will take vengeance on him, and Allah is all-mighty, wielder of retribution." (5:95).

These are diverse instances of Divine pardon (العفو الإلهي), and we have explained the particularity of each in the commentary on the verses containing them; thus, one should refer back to those sections.

Not of the same category as the aforementioned is His saying, the Exalted: **عَفَا اللَّهُ عَنكَ لِمَ أَذِنْتَ لَهُمْ** — "May Allah excuse you! Why did you grant them leave?" (9:43). This is a supplication (دعاء) similar to our saying: "May Allah forgive you, why did you do such and such?" A similar construction, though in the opposite sense, is His saying: **إِنَّهُ فَكَّرَ وَقَدَّرَ * فَقُتِلَ كَيْفَ قَدَّرَ** — "Indeed he reflected and scheme. May he be perished! How he schemed!" (74:18-19). Also not of that category is His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُّبِينًا * لِّيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِن ذَنبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ** — "Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory, that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come." (48:1-2). This is evidenced by making forgiveness (المغفرة) a consequence and an end (غاية) resulting from His opening of Mecca for His Prophet. There is no inherent link between the forgiveness of sin (الذنب) in the sense of a transgression (الإثم) and a military victory (الفتح). The full discussion on this will follow in its proper place, if Allah wills.

**4 - Pardon has levels:**
Since pardon (العفو) and forgiveness (المغفرة) pertain to sin (الذنب) which entails some form of recompense (المجازاة) and punishment (العقاب), and since recompense—as you have learned—has a vast range and various scattered levels, pardon follows it in the diversity of its levels accordingly. This variation does not occur in the sin itself—meaning the evil consequence (التبعة السيئة) that the action entails—for variation in that regard is undeniable, and recompense, whether punishment or reward, is measured by its weight.

Therefore, what is unavoidable in our present inquiry is to investigate sin and its different levels, and to reflect upon what the primordial intellect (العقل الفطري) leads to. Although this is a Quranic inquiry intended to attain what the Divine Book holds regarding these realities, the Exalted—as He has clarified in His speech—addresses us according to the capacity of our intellects and by the primordial standards (الموازين الفطرية) with which we weigh things in the stages of theory and practice. This has been pointed out in several places in the discussions of this book. In various instances of His explanations, the Exalted has sought aid from human intellect and thought, supporting the objectives of His speech by saying: **أَفَلَا تَعْقِلُونَ** — "Do you not exercise your reason?" and **أَفَلَا تَتَفَكَّرُونَ** — "Do you not reflect?", and similar expressions.

What sound consideration (الاعتبار الصحيح) yields is that the first thing human society concerns itself with and respects is practical laws (الأحكام العملية) and respected traditions (السنن المحترمة) which, through their implementation and persistence, preserve human objectives and guide society toward its happiness in life. Subsequently, it establishes penal laws (أحكام جزائية) according to which the disobedient violator of social laws is punished, and the obedient, compliant individual is rewarded.

In this stage, nothing is called a sin (الذنب) except the violation of the core practical laws. The number of sins necessarily corresponds to the number of articles in the social laws. This is also what is firmly rooted in the minds of us Muslims regarding the meaning of the word "sin" (الذنب) and related terms like "misdeed" (السيئة), "disobedience" (المعصية), "transgression" (الإثم), "error" (الخطيئة), "grave sin" (الحوب), "depravity" (الفسق), and the like.

However, the matter does not stop at this limit. When practical laws are acted upon, monitored, and preserved, they lead society toward ethics (أخلاق) and traits suitable for them and harmonious with the objectives of society, which are the goal of their association. These ethics are what society calls human virtues (الفضائل الإنسانية), which it covets and encourages, while their opposites are vices (الرذائل). Although these vary according to the traditions and objectives of different societies, the fact that social laws produce them is an inescapable reality.

These virtuous ethics, though they are spiritual attributes (أوصاف روحية) with no guarantee of implementation in the practical sphere of societies, and though they are not directly voluntary (غير اختيارية) because they are ingrained habits (ملكات), they are considered voluntary by virtue of their preliminaries—namely, the repetition of action. This is because their realization follows the repeated performance of prescribed laws or the repeated violation of them. In their regard, one can conceive of intellectual commands (أوامر عقلية) pertaining to virtuous ethics such as courage, chastity, and justice, and intellectual prohibitions (نواه عقلية) deterring from base ethics such as cowardice, recklessness, apathy, greed, and injustice. Likewise, one can conceive for them a punishment and reward termed intellectual punishment and reward (العقاب والثواب العقليين), such as praise and blame.

In short, a level of sin (الذنب) is realized here above the previously mentioned level: the level of failing to fulfill ethical laws and the intellectual commands related to them. These intellectual commands are only considered "commands" due to the necessary correlation (التلازم) between the obligatory actions they lead to and the ethics themselves. Thus, there is a judge who rules with obligation and commands it, which is the human intellect (العقل الإنساني); the same applies to calling intellectual prohibitions "prohibitions." This is our habit in all cases of necessary correlation: if we assume the performance of one of two correlated things, we immediately command the performance of the other and its obligation, and we view the failure to do so as disobedience (عصيان) to this intellectual command and a sin deserving of some form of censure.

From here, another matter becomes apparent: since these virtues contain obligations that must be adopted—and similarly, vices contain prohibitions—and also contain recommended (مندوبة) and desirable matters that are like an ornament or a beautiful form within them—these being the refined manners (الآداب الحسنة) to which intellectual commands of approval pertain—if we assume a certain context (ظرف) for an individual, the manners correlated with that context, though recommended in themselves, become intellectually mandatory (مأمور به عقلا أمرا إيجابيا) to fulfill the right of that assumed context.

For example, a desert dweller living a nomadic tribal life, because his life's context is far from the average standard of urban life, is only held accountable for the essential social laws and general traditions that his intellect and understanding can grasp. He might commit a shameful act or utter a crude word, yet the urbanite overlooks it, excusing him due to his deficiency in understanding and his distance from the great populace where the repeated observation of customs and manners is the best teacher.

Furthermore, the average urban person is not held accountable for what the rare, exceptional individuals of society—those of subtle understanding and refined grace—are held accountable for. There is no excuse for the average person's omission of the subtleties of etiquette or the refinements of speech and action, except that his understanding is commensurate with what he produces; he does not perceive the requirements of etiquette beyond what he performs, and his context is his context.

However, what the average person does that is inappropriate is something for which the exceptional men (الأوحديون) are held accountable. They might be taken to task for a subtle slip in speech, a slight hesitation in movement, an imperceptible loss of a moment in stillness, a glance, or the closing of an eye, and such. All of this is considered a sin (ذنب) for them, though it is not a sin in the sense of violating legal statutes, whether religious or worldly. It has become famous among them that: "The good deeds of the righteous are the misdeeds of the near ones" (حسنات الأبرار سيئات المقربين).

Whenever the path becomes more refined and the station more subtle, hidden sins appear there which were ignored before this context was realized; they were not felt by the person charged with duties, nor were they penalized by the authority of reckoning.

This culminates—according to precise investigation—in the rulings arising in the contexts of love (الحب) and hatred (البغض). You see the eye of the hater—especially in a state of anger—viewing all good deeds as evil and blameworthy. Conversely, the lover, when lost in passion and immersed in infatuation, views the slightest heart-felt negligence toward his beloved as a great sin, even if he attends to the actions of the limbs with all their pillars. This is only because he sees the value of his actions in the path of love as being in proportion to the orientation of his soul and the attraction of his heart toward his beloved. If he is cut off from Him by a moment of heart-felt negligence, he has turned away from the Beloved, ceased His remembrance, and thereby nullified the purity of his heart.

Even preoccupation with the necessities of life, such as eating and drinking, is considered by him as a crime and disobedience. This is because, although the act itself is a necessity to which a human is compelled, each of these compelled acts is, in its origin, voluntary in itself. Occupying oneself with it is occupying oneself with other than the Beloved and a voluntary turning away from Him, which constitutes a sin. Therefore, we see those possessed by infatuation and passion, as well as the sorrowful and dejected, recoiling from preoccupation with eating, drinking, and the like.

In a similar vein, one should interpret what is narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in his saying: **إنه ليغان على قلبي فأستغفر الله كل يوم سبعين مرة** — "Indeed, my heart becomes clouded, so I seek forgiveness from Allah seventy times every day." Upon this, one can also interpret His saying, the Exalted: **وَاسْتَغْفِرْ لِذَنبِكَ وَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكَارِ** — "And plead for forgiveness for your sin, and celebrate the praise of your Lord morning and evening." (40:55), and His saying: **فَسَبِّحْ بِحَمْدِ رَبِّكَ وَاسْتَغْفِرْهُ ۚ إِنَّهُ كَانَ تَوَّابًا** — "Then celebrate the praise of your Lord, and plead to Him for forgiveness; indeed He is all-clement." (110:3).

Upon this is also interpreted what the Exalted narrated from several of His noble prophets, such as the saying of Noah: **رَّبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيَّ وَلِمَن دَخَلَ بَيْتِيَ مُؤْمِنًا** — "My Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and whoever enters my house in faith" (71:28); and the saying of Abraham: **رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسَابُ** — "Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and all the faithful, on the day when the reckoning is held." (14:41); and the saying of Moses for himself and his brother: **رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِأَخِي وَأَدْخِلْنَا فِي رَحْمَتِكَ** — "My Lord, forgive me and my brother, and admit us into Your mercy" (7:151); and what was narrated from the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله): **سَمِعْنَا وَأَطَعْنَا ۖ غُفْرَانَكَ رَبَّنَا وَإِلَيْكَ الْمَصِيرُ** — "We hear and obey. Our Lord, forgive us, and toward You is the return." (2:285).

For the prophets (عليه‌السلام), despite their infallibility (العصمة), it is impossible for disobedience to issue from them, or for them to commit a sin (الذنب) in the sense of violating a religious statute which they were sent to call toward, and which they established through word and deed, and whose obedience was made mandatory by Allah. There is no meaning in mandating obedience to one from whom the occurrence of disobedience is not deemed impossible; exalted is Allah far above that.

Similarly, this category includes what is narrated from some of them (عليه‌السلام) regarding the admission of wrongdoing (الظلم) and the like, such as the saying of Dhu’l-Nun: **لَّا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا أَنتَ سُبْحَانَكَ إِنِّي كُنتُ مِنَ الظَّالِمِينَ** — "There is no god except You! Immaculate are You! I have indeed been among the wrongdoers!" (21:87). Just as it is permissible for them to count some of their permissible (مباحة) actions as a sin for themselves and seek forgiveness from Allah, the Glorified, it is likewise permissible to count it as wrongdoing (ظلم) against themselves, for every sin is a wrongdoing.

It has been previously mentioned that there is another interpretation: that the intended "wrongdoing" is wrongdoing against the self (الظلم على النفس), as in the saying of Adam and his spouse: **رَبَّنَا ظَلَمْنَا أَنفُسَنَا وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الْخَاسِرِينَ** — "Our Lord, we have wronged ourselves! If You do not forgive us and have mercy on us, we will surely be among the losers." (7:23).

Beware of imagining that our saying regarding a verse that it has "such and such an interpretation" is an admission that it contradicts the apparent meaning (ظاهر الكلام), followed by an effort to find a different meaning to impose on the speech and apply to the Quranic verses to preserve sectarian views or out of partisan necessity.

We have already conducted a free inquiry into the infallibility of the prophets (عليه‌السلام) by contemplating the verses themselves, without relying on foreign or external premises, in the second volume of this book.

We have clarified there and elsewhere that the apparent meaning (ظاهر الكلام) is not limited in its identification to the common understanding (الفهم العامي) pertaining to the specific sentence under discussion. Rather, situational and textual contexts (القرائن المقامية والكلامية), whether connected or disconnected—such as a verse addressing the meaning of another verse—have a decisive impact on the apparent meanings, especially in the Divine Speech, parts of which explain others, witness for others, and confirm others.

Negligence of this point is what led many exegetes and theologians to invent "interpretation" (التأويل) in the sense of diverting speech to what contradicts its apparent meaning, and resorting to it in verses that they perceived as contradicting their specific school of thought. You see them cutting the Quran into pieces and then interpreting each piece according to what a common person in the marketplace understands from similar marketplace speech. When they hear the Exalted saying: **فَظَنَّ أَن لَّن نَّقْدِرَ عَلَيْهِ** — "and he thought We would not have power over him" (21:87), they interpret it to mean that he (عليه‌السلام)—far be it from him—imagined or was certain that Allah, the Glorified, was incapable of seizing him. This is despite the fact that the following verse: **وَكَذَٰلِكَ نُنجِي الْمُؤْمِنِينَ** — "and thus do We deliver the faithful" (21:88) counts him among the believers, and there is no faith for one who doubts Allah's power, let alone one who deems His incapacity likely or certain.

When they hear the Exalted saying: **لِّيَغْفِرَ لَكَ اللَّهُ مَا تَقَدَّمَ مِن ذَنبِكَ وَمَا تَأَخَّرَ** — "that Allah may forgive you what is past of your sin and what is to come" (48:2), they understand from it that he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sinned and Allah forgave him, just as one of us sins by violating a command or prohibition from Allah that constitutes a secondary jurisprudential issue.

Contemplation did not guide them even to the extent of referring to the preceding part of the verse: **إِنَّا فَتَحْنَا لَكَ فَتْحًا مُّبِينًا** — "Indeed We have inaugurated for you a clear victory" (48:1), so it might become clear to them that if this sin and the forgiveness related to it were like our sins and the forgiveness related to them, there would be no reason to link forgiveness to the conquest of Mecca as an end (غاية) linked to its means. Likewise, there would be no reason for the subsequent conjunction: **وَيُتِمَّ نِعْمَتَهُ عَلَيْكَ وَيَهْدِيَكَ صِرَاطًا مُّسْتَقِيمًا * وَيَنصُرَكَ اللَّهُ نَصْرًا عَزِيزًا** — "and complete His blessing upon you and guide you on a straight path, and that Allah may help you with a mighty help." (48:2-3).

Similarly, when they hear other verses containing what they perceive as the slips of the prophets, such as those in the stories of Adam, Noah, Abraham, Lot, Jacob, Joseph, David, Solomon, Job, and Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وعليهم), they hasten to impugn the sanctuary of their purity. They do not shrink from showing disrespect toward them, though they themselves are more deserving of what they cast, and there is no stain like disrespect.

Their ill fortune and poor insight led them to replace their Lord, the Lord of all the worlds, with a lord described by the distorted Torah and Gospels—a personified hidden power that manages the wheel of existence as a human tyrant manages his kingdom, with no concern but to satisfy the tyranny of his desire and anger. Thus, they were ignorant of the station of their Lord, then they forgot the station of prophethood and ignored their high, noble spiritual ranks and their lofty real stations. Consequently, those pure, sacred souls became, in their view, similar to base, vile souls that have nothing of human honor except the name; one kills for this very reason, another betrays that person's honor, and a third covets his wealth. Yet, despite their ignorance, they would not accept such scandals in one who manages any of their worldly affairs or stands one day to manage the interests of their house and family. How then do they accept attributing these scandals to Allah, the Glorified, the All-Knowing, the All-Wise, who sent His messengers to His servants so they would have no argument after them? I wonder, what argument could be established against a disbeliever or a profligate if it were permissible for the messenger to disbelieve, commit immorality, or call to polytheism and idolatry, then disavow it and attribute it to Satan?

When they are reminded of some of the Divine infallibility (العصمة الإلهية), bestowed stations, and spiritual positions of Allah's prophets (عليه‌السلام), they count that as polytheism (شرك) toward Allah and exaggeration (غلو) regarding Allah's servants, and they begin reciting His saying: **قُلْ إِنَّمَا أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِّثْلُكُمْ** — "Say, ‘I am just a human being like you.’"

They are correct in their rejection in one sense: for what they conceive of the Lord, exalted is His Name, and the attributes they describe Him with in His essence and acts are lower than the stations of the prophets (عليه‌السلام) they are reminded of, and inferior in rank and value. All of this is among the calamities that Islam and its people have faced from what the People of the Book, especially the Jews, inserted into the narrations and what their hands wrought. They turned the wheel on a different axis and believed that Allah, the Glorified, whom nothing is like, is like a tyrannical human who sees himself as free and unaccountable for what he does while they are the ones questioned. They believed that the sequence of effects from their causes, the production of results from premises, and the requirement of existential characteristics—whether formal or conceptual—for their effects are all arbitrary, not due to a real link.

They believed that Allah sealed prophethood with Muhammad and sent down the Quran to him, and singled out Moses for speaking and Jesus for support with the Spirit, not because of any particularity in their noble souls, but simply because He willed to single them out for such and such. They believed that Moses' striking the stone with his staff so that it gushed forth was like one of us striking a stone with a staff, except that Allah makes that one gush and not this one; and that Jesus' saying to the dead, "Arise by Allah's permission," is like one of us calling out among the graves, "Arise by Allah's permission," except that Allah revives those and not these, and so on.

All of this is nothing but an analogy of the system of creation (نظام التكوين) to the system of legislation (نظام التشريع), which has no foundation except convention, terminology, and agreement, which does not exceed the scope of social context nor go beyond the world of socialized man.

Had they reflected a little and contemplated the various verses addressing the matter of sin (الذنب) and disobedience (المعصية) in the technical sense—which is the violation of a masterly command or prohibition—they would have realized that there is a forgiveness (مغفرة) that is above the well-known forgiveness.

For Allah, the Glorified, repeats in His speech that He has servants whom He calls the "sincere ones" (المخلصين), who are protected from disobedience and in whom Satan has no hope. Thus, they have no sin—in the well-known sense—and no need for the forgiveness related to such sin. He has explicitly stated regarding several of His prophets, such as Abraham, Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, and Moses, that they are sincere ones (مخلصون), as in His saying regarding Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob: **إِنَّا أَخْلَصْنَاهُم بِخَالِصَةٍ ذِكْرَى الدَّارِ** — "Indeed We purified them with a quality of pure devotion: the remembrance of the [ultimate] abode." (38:46); and His saying regarding Joseph: **إِنَّهُ مِنْ عِبَادِنَا الْمُخْلَصِينَ** — "Indeed he was one of Our dedicated servants." (12:24); and His saying regarding Moses: **إِنَّهُ كَانَ مُخْلَصًا** — "Indeed he was a dedicated servant" (19:51). Yet He narrated from them the request for forgiveness, such as the saying of Abraham (عليه‌السلام): **رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيَّ** — "Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents" (14:41); and the saying of Moses (عليه‌السلام): **رَبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِأَخِي وَأَدْخِلْنَا فِي رَحْمَتِكَ** — "My Lord, forgive me and my brother, and admit us into Your mercy" (7:151). If forgiveness only pertained to sin in the well-known sense, this would not be consistent.

Yes, someone might say: "They (عليه‌السلام) count themselves as sinners out of humility toward Allah, the Glorified, while they have no sin." However, this speaker should realize that they (عليه‌السلام) did not err in this view of theirs, nor did they speak arbitrarily; for the inclusion of forgiveness for them has a correct meaning, and the matter is serious.

Furthermore, in the supplication of Abraham (عليه‌السلام): **رَبَّنَا اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيَّ وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ يَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْحِسَابُ** — "Our Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and all the faithful, on the day when the reckoning is held" (14:41), there is a prayer for all the believers—including the sincere ones—for forgiveness. Likewise, in the supplication of Noah (عليه‌السلام): **رَّبِّ اغْفِرْ لِي وَلِوَالِدَيَّ وَلِمَن دَخَلَ بَيْتِيَ مُؤْمِنًا وَلِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ وَالْمُؤْمِنَاتِ** — "My Lord! Forgive me and my parents, and whoever enters my house in faith, and the faithful men and women" (71:28), there is an inclusion by its absolute nature of the sincere ones. There is no meaning in seeking forgiveness for one who has no sin requiring forgiveness.

All of this alerts us that among the sins to which forgiveness pertains is that which is other than sin in the conventional sense, and likewise, among forgiveness is that which is other than forgiveness in its conventional sense. Allah, the Exalted, has narrated from Abraham his saying: **وَالَّذِي أَطْمَعُ أَن يَغْفِرَ لِي خَطِيئَتِي يَوْمَ الدِّينِ** — "and who, I hope, will forgive me my fault on the Day of Retribution." (26:82). Perhaps this is the reason for what we observe: that in several places in His speech, when He mentions mercy (الرحمة) or the otherworldly mercy which is Paradise, He precedes it with the mention of forgiveness, such as His saying: **وَقُل رَّبِّ اغْفِرْ وَارْحَمْ** — "And say, ‘My Lord, forgive and have mercy’" (23:118); and His saying: **وَاغْفِرْ لَنَا وَارْحَمْنَا** — "and forgive us and have mercy upon us" (2:286); and His saying narrating from Adam and his spouse: **وَإِن لَّمْ تَغْفِرْ لَنَا وَتَرْحَمْنَا** — "If You do not forgive us and have mercy on us" (7:23); and His saying regarding Noah (عليه‌السلام): **وَإِلَّا تَغْفِرْ لِي وَتَرْحَمْنِي** — "and if You do not forgive me and have mercy on me" (11:47).

It follows from the previous explanation that sin (الذنب) has different levels arranged vertically (طولا), just as forgiveness (المغفرة) has levels corresponding to them; each level of forgiveness pertains to its corresponding level of sin. It is not necessary that every sin and error be related to a masterly command or prohibition so that simple, common understandings can identify and perceive it, nor that every forgiveness be related to this type of sin.

What has become clear to us regarding the levels of sin and forgiveness according to the previous general inquiry are four levels:

First: The sin related to masterly commands and prohibitions, which is the violation of a secondary or primary religious ruling. If you generalize the expression, you could say: the violation of any article of legal statutes, whether religious or non-religious. A forgiveness corresponding to it in rank pertains to it.

Second: The sin related to intellectual ethical rulings (الحكم العقلي الخلقي) and the forgiveness related to it.

Third: The sin related to refined manners (الحكم الأدبي) for one whose life's context is that of etiquette, and the forgiveness related to it. These two categories might not be counted as sins and forgivenesses according to common understanding, or they might consider them as such metaphorically (مجازا). However, it is not metaphorical at all, because of the real effects that follow them, as you have learned.

Fourth: The sin judged by the taste of love (ذوق الحب) and the forgiveness related to it. In the context of hatred, there is also something similar to them. This type is not counted by common understanding as one of the categories, and they have erred in that—not due to injustice in their judgment, but due to the deficiency of their understanding in conceptualizing it and identifying its meaning.

One of them might say: "It is among the delusions of lovers and the delirious, or a poetic imagination that does not lean on an intellectual reality." He has neglected that these conceptions, while they are delusions and imaginations in the path of social life, return as realities—and what realities!—in the path of servitude (العبودية) arising from a Divine love that melts the heart and infatuates the mind, leaving the human with no perception of anything other than his Lord, and no will by which he desires except what He desires.

At that point, it appears to him that a slight turn of his toward himself or toward his desire for something is a great sin and a thick veil (حجاب) that nothing but Divine forgiveness can remove. Allah, the Glorified, has counted sin as a veil for the heart against complete orientation toward its Lord, as He said: **كَلَّا ۖ بَلْ ۜ رَانَ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِم مَّا كَانُوا يَكْسِبُونَ * كَلَّا إِنَّهُمْ عَن رَّبِّهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لَّمَحْجُوبُونَ** — "No indeed! Rather their hearts have been sullied by what they have been earning. No indeed! They will be barred from their Lord on that day." (83:14-15).

This is what serious inquiry, which does not play with realities, yields. It may be possible for the friends of Allah (أولياء الله) who walk the path of His love in their servitude to perceive subtleties of sin and refinements of forgiveness that the hands of general, universal inquiries can hardly reach.

**5 - Does censure or forgiveness necessitate a sin?**
The researcher into the custom of rational people (العقلاء) in society finds that they base censure (المؤأخذة) and punishment (العقاب) upon voluntary duty (التكليف الاختياري). Among the conditions for its validity in their view is intellect (العقل). There are other conditions whose origins, definitions, and limits vary across societies, which we are not concerned with here.

Our discussion is regarding the intellect (العقل), which is the power of distinguishing between the beautiful and the ugly, the beneficial and the harmful, and the good and the evil, according to the average state of people in their society. For people, from a social perspective, see that in the human being there is an active principle of this nature, even if scientific research might lead to the conclusion that it is not a natural power deposited in the human like the imaginative or retentive powers, but rather a habit (ملكة) resulting from the harmony of several powers in action, like justice.

Societies, despite their differences, see that duty is contingent upon this thing called "intellect." Consequently, reward and punishment, which follow duty, necessarily follow it; thus, the rational person is rewarded for his obedience and punished for his crime.

As for the non-rational, such as the child, the insane, the simpleton, and every other oppressed person (مستضعف), there is no reward or punishment for the obedience or disobedience they perform in the true sense of reward and punishment. However, they might be rewarded for their obedience with an encouraging reward or censured and disciplined for their crime with what is called punishment as a form of discipline (تأديبا). This is common and widespread in societies, including Islamic society.

These individuals, with respect to the happiness (السعادة) and wretchedness (الشقاوة) acquired through the fulfillment or violation of duties in the worldly life, are neither happy nor wretched. Since they have no duty, there is no reward for them to be happy with, nor punishment for them to be wretched by, even if they are encouraged with good or disciplined with evil.

As for the Hereafter, which the Divine religion establishes and then divides people into two groups with no third: the happy and the wretched, or the rewarded and the punished—what the Noble Quran mentions in that regard is a general matter whose details are not clarified. This is because there is no intellectual way to identify the details of their states after this world. The Exalted said: **وَآخَرُونَ مُرْجَوْنَ لِأَمْرِ اللَّهِ إِمَّا يُعَذِّبُهُمْ وَإِمَّا يَتُوبُ عَلَيْهِمْ ۗ وَاللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ** — "And [there are] others who are deferred to Allah’s command: either He will punish them, or He will turn to them [mercifully]. And Allah is all-knowing, all-wise." (9:106). And the Exalted said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ ظَالِمِي أَنفُسِهِمْ قَالُوا فِيمَ كُنتُمْ ۖ قَالُوا كُنَّا مُسْتَضْعَفِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ ۚ قَالُوا أَلَمْ تَكُنْ أَرْضُ اللَّهِ وَاسِعَةً فَتُهَاجِرُوا فِيهَا ۚ فَأُولَٰئِكَ مَأْوَاهُمْ جَهَنَّمُ ۖ وَسَاءَتْ مَصِيرًا * إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ مِنَ الرِّجَالِ وَالنِّسَاءِ وَالْوِلْدَانِ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ حِيلَةً وَلَا يَهْتَدُونَ سَبِيلًا * فَأُولَٰئِكَ عَسَى اللَّهُ أَن يَعْفُوَ عَنْهُمْ ۚ وَكَانَ اللَّهُ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا** — "Indeed, those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves, they [the angels] say, ‘What state were you in?’ They say, ‘We were abased in the land.’ They say, ‘Was not Allah’s earth vast so that you might have migrated in it?’ The abode of such shall be hell, and it is an evil destination. Except the abased among men, women and children, who have neither the resources nor are they guided to any way. Maybe Allah will excuse them, for Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving." (4:97-99).

The verses—as you see—include pardon (العفو) for them and turning toward them (التوبة عليهم), and there is no forgiveness (مغفرة) in a case where there is no sin. They also include their punishment (عذابهم), and there is no punishment for one who has no duty. However, you have learned that sin, as well as forgiveness, punishment, and reward, possess different levels: some pertain to the violation of a masterly or intellectual duty, and some pertain to base psychological states (الهيئات النفسانية) and the impurities of the heart (أدران القلب) that veil the human from his Lord. These individuals, though they are removed from the application of duty which depends on intellect, are not immune to the defilements of the soul and the coverings of the heart, which require removal, pardon, concealment, and forgiveness to enjoy the bliss of proximity and presence in the Sanctuary of Holiness.

Perhaps this is what is intended by what was narrated in some reports: "That Allah, the Glorified, will gather them—then He will create a fire and command them to enter it. Whoever enters it enters Paradise, and whoever refuses to enter it enters the Fire." The discussion regarding the narrations will follow in the exegesis of Surah al-Tawbah, if Allah wills, and some discussion has already passed in Surah al-Nisa.

Among the uses of pardon (العفو) and forgiveness (المغفرة) in other than the case of sin in His speech, the Exalted, is what repeatedly occurs in the case of lifting a ruling, as in His saying: **فَمَنِ اضْطُرَّ فِي مَخْمَصَةٍ غَيْرَ مُتَجَانِفٍ لِّإِثْمٍ ۙ فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ** — "But should anyone be compelled by hunger, without inclining to sin, then Allah is indeed all-forgiving, all-merciful." (5:3); and similar to it is what is in Surah al-An'am. Also, His saying, the Exalted, regarding the lifting of ablution (الوضوء) for one who lacks water: **وَإِن كُنتُم مَّرْضَىٰ أَوْ عَلَىٰ سَفَرٍ... فَتَيَمَّمُوا صَعِيدًا طَيِّبًا فَامْسَحُوا بِوُجُوهِكُمْ وَأَيْدِيكُمْ ۗ إِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ عَفُوًّا غَفُورًا** — "and if you are sick or on a journey... then find some clean ground and wipe your faces and hands. Indeed Allah is all-excusing, all-forgiving." (4:43). And His saying regarding the penalty for those who spread corruption in the land: **إِلَّا الَّذِينَ تَابُوا مِن قَبْلِ أَن تَقْدِرُوا عَلَيْهِمْ ۖ فَاعْلَمُوا أَنَّ اللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ** — "excepting those who repent before you capture them, and know that Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful." (5:34). And His saying regarding the lifting of the duty of struggle (الجهاد) from those with excuses: **مَا عَلَى الْمُحْسِنِينَ مِن سَبِيلٍ ۚ وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ** — "there is no way [to blame] the virtuous, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful." (9:91), among others.

The Exalted said regarding the trials and calamities that afflict people: **وَمَا أَصَابَكُم مِّن مُّصِيبَةٍ فَبِمَا كَسَبَتْ أَيْدِيكُمْ وَيَعْفُو عَن كَثِيرٍ** — "Whatever affliction that may visit you is because of what your hands have earned, and He excuses many [an offense]." (42:30).

It is revealed through this that the attributes of pardon (العفو) and forgiveness (المغفرة) from Him, the Exalted, like the attributes of mercy (الرحمة) and guidance (الهداية), pertain to both existential (تكوينية) and legislative (تشريعية) matters. Thus, He, the Exalted, pardons sins and acts of disobedience, erasing them from the record of deeds; He pardons a ruling that has a cause necessitating its establishment, erasing it so He does not legislate it; and He pardons trials and calamities whose causes are present, erasing them so they do not afflict the human.

**6 - The link between action and recompense:**
We have learned in the preceding discussion that masterly commands and prohibitions—the laws circulating among people—entail beautiful, good effects upon their fulfillment, which is reward (الثواب), and evil effects upon their violation and rebellion, which is called punishment (العقاب). This is like a device they employ to ensure action. Their establishment of a good recompense for compliance is only to serve as an incentive for the actor, and the evil recompense for violation is so the actor remains in fear and caution against rebellion.

From here, it becomes apparent that the link between action and recompense is a conventional, established link (رابطة جعلية وضعية) from society or the authority. They were driven to this establishment by their dire need for the action, to benefit from it, fulfill a need, and close a gap. Therefore, you see that when they become self-sufficient and their need for the action is removed, they become lenient in fulfilling what they pledged of reward and punishment.

For this reason also, you see the recompense varying in quantity and the wage differing in intensity and weakness according to the variation in the need for the action. Whenever the need increases, the wage increases, and whenever it decreases, it decreases. Thus, the commander, the commanded, the one charged with duty, and the one charging it are like the seller and the buyer: each gives something and takes something.

The wage and reward are like the price (الثمن), and the punishment is like the liability (الدرك) upon one who destroys something, thus guaranteeing its value, which remains as a debt in his responsibility.

In short, it is a conventional, conceptual matter (أمر وضعي اعتباري) similar to all other social titles, rulings, and standards upon which the wheel of human society turns, such as leadership and followership, command and prohibition, obedience and disobedience, obligation and prohibition, ownership and wealth, buying and selling, and others. Realities (الحقائق), however, are the external existents and the events surrounding them, whose state does not change by wealth or poverty, honor or humiliation, praise or blame—such as the earth and what comes out of it, death and life, health and sickness, hunger and satiety, thirst and quenching.

This is what is held by the rational people of society. Allah, the Glorified, has addressed us in His speech in a manner similar to how we address one another. He cast our happiness, to which He guides us through His religion, into the mold of social traditions. Thus, He commanded and prohibited, encouraged and cautioned, gave glad tidings and warned, and promised reward and threatened punishment. Consequently, we began to receive the religion in the easiest way we receive social traditions and laws. The Exalted said: **وَلَوْلَا فَضْلُ اللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ وَرَحْمَتُهُ مَا زَكَىٰ مِنكُم مِّنْ أَحَدٍ أَبَدًا** — "Were it not for Allah’s grace and His mercy upon you, not one of you would ever be pure." (24:21).

Yet He, the Glorified, did not neglect the instruction of souls prepared to perceive realities. He pointed out in verses of His speech that behind these religious sciences contained in the apparent meanings of the Book and the Sunnah is a greater matter, and a secret that is more precious and more splendid. The Exalted said: **وَمَا هَٰذِهِ الْحَيَاةُ الدُّنْيَا إِلَّا لَهْوٌ وَلَعِبٌ ۚ وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ** — "The life of this world is nothing but diversion and play, but the abode of the Hereafter is indeed Life [itself]." (29:64).

He thus considered the life of this world (الحياة الدنيا) to be a play (لعب) with no structure other than imagination, and no function other than to distract man from what truly concerns him; whereas the Hereafter (الدار الآخرة) and the eternal felicity of man are what possess the reality of life. If what is meant by "the life of this world" is the very essence of what we call life, excluding the vital affairs (الشئون الحيوية) attached to it—such as wealth, status, dominion, might, honor, and the like—then its being a play and a diversion (لهو), despite the realities we perceive within it, necessitates that these vital affairs be play and diversion by priority (بطريق أولى). If, however, what is meant is the worldly life with all its attachments, then the matter is even clearer.

These social conventions (السنن الاجتماعية) and the objectives sought through them—such as might, status, wealth, and others—as well as the subjects and objectives contained within religious instruction, toward which Allah, the Glorified, has guided us through the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and then through the Message (الرسالة), are like the play established by a wise, nurturing guardian (الولي المربي) for a small child who cannot distinguish his benefit from his harm, or his good from his evil. The guardian then joins him in it to exercise his body, relax his mind, and prepare him for a system of work and the pursuit of success through it. Thus, the playful action that occurs is, on the part of the child, a beautiful play that guides him toward the threshold of serious work; but on the part of the guardian, it is wisdom (حكمة) and serious action (عمل جدي) that is in no way part of play.

The Exalted said: **وَمَا خَلَقْنَا السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَمَا بَيْنَهُمَا لَاعِبِينَ مَا خَلَقْنَاهُمَا إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ** "We did not create the heavens and the earth and whatever is between them in play. We did not create them except with consummate truth, but most of them do not know." (44:38-39). This verse is close in meaning to the preceding one.

Then the Exalted explained how this formal upbringing leads to its spiritual objectives through a general parable He struck for mankind, saying: **أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِها فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَداً رابِياً وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِثْلُهُ كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْباطِلَ فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفاءً وَأَمَّا ما يَنْفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ** "He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to [the extent of] their capacity, and the flood carries off a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire to make ornaments or housewares, there is a similar scum. That is how Allah compares truth and falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits the people, it remains in the earth." (13:17).

It has thus become clear from His explanation, the Exalted, that between the deed and the recompense (الجزاء) there is a real connection (رابطة حقيقية) beyond the conventional, nominal connection (الرابطة الوضعية الاعتبارية) that exists between them among members of society, and upon which the outward aspect of His divine teaching proceeds.

**7 - The deed conveys the connection to the soul:**
Then the Exalted explained that the deed conveys this connection to the soul (النفس) by way of the psychic disposition (الهيأة النفسانية) that the soul acquires from the deed and the state (الحالة) to which the deed leads it. The Exalted said: **وَلَكِنْ يُؤَاخِذُكُمْ بِمَا كَسَبَتْ قُلُوبُكُمْ** "but He will take you to task for what your hearts have earned." (2:225). And He said: **وَإِنْ تُبْدُوا مَا فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحَاسِبْكُمْ بِهِ اللَّهُ** "Whether you disclose what is in your souls or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it." (2:284). There are many other verses to this effect.

It becomes clear through these that all the effects resulting from deeds, whether reward (ثواب) or punishment (عقاب), actually result from what the souls earn by way of the deeds, and that the deeds serve only as intermediaries (الوساطة).

Then the Exalted explained that the recompense they will face for their deeds is, in reality, the deeds themselves; it is not as when a human being establishes a deed in his society and then follows it with a separate recompense. Rather, the deed is preserved with Allah, the Glorified, through the preservation of the performing soul, and then Allah manifests it to the soul on the day when the secrets are examined. Allah the Exalted said: **يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَا عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَرًا وَمَا عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَهُ أَمَدًا بَعِيدًا** "The day when every soul will find present whatever good it has done; and as for the evil it has done, it will wish there were a great distance between it and that [evil]." (3:30). And the Exalted said: **لَا تَعْتَذِرُوا الْيَوْمَ إِنَّمَا تُجْزَوْنَ مَا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** "Do not make any excuses today. You are only being requited for what you used to do." (66:7). The indication of these verses is manifest, and many other verses are joined with them in this regard.

Among those with the finest indication is His saying, the Exalted: **لَقَدْ كُنْتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِنْ هَذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنْكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ** "You were certainly oblivious of this. We have removed from you your veil, and so your sight is sharp today." (50:22). This is an allusion to the station of the present recompense, and He considered him "oblivious" (غافلاً) of it in the world by the context of His saying: "today" (اليوم). There is no meaning to "obliviousness" (الغفلة) except regarding an existing matter. Then He mentioned the "removal of his veil" (كشف غطائه) from him, and there is no reason for a veil (الغطاء) unless there is something being veiled. Thus, what he encounters and perceives of the recompense on the Day of Resurrection was present and existing in the world, except that it had not been unveiled.

These verses interpret the other verses that appear to suggest a separation between the deed and the recompense, because the verses of recompense look toward the stage of the conventional social connection (الرابطة الاجتماعية الوضعية), while these verses look toward the stage of the real connection (الرابطة الحقيقية) as we have explained. We have touched upon this discussion to some extent in the commentary (تفسير) on His saying, the Exalted: **خَتَمَ اللَّهُ عَلَى قُلُوبِهِمْ** "Allah has set a seal on their hearts" (2:7) in the first volume of the book; so let whoever wishes refer to it. And Allah is the Guide.

(Completed, and praise be to Allah).
